176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188	
189	\subsection{Stages 5a/5b: K\"ahler-differentials localisation substrate (iter-193)}
190	\label{subsec:kaehler_localisation_substrate}
191	
192	The Jacobian-criterion direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}
193	(\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} below) factors on the Lean side
194	through a six-stage chain. Stages~1--2 (smooth \(\Rightarrow\) flat at a
195	stalk; smooth \(\Rightarrow\) admits a standard-smooth presentation
196	locally) are axiom-clean since iter-191; Stages~3--4 (the
197	scheme-to-algebra bridge for standard-smooth presentations; freeness of
198	\(\Omega_{S/R}\) for a standard-smooth algebra \(R \to S\)) are
199	axiom-clean since iter-192. The remaining Stages~5--6 reduce the
200	Stacks~00TT conclusion to a freeness plus rank computation on K\"ahler
201	differentials, transported through localisation at the prime defining
202	the stalk. Iter-193 packaged Stages~5a (transport of freeness) and 5b
203	(matching rank under localisation) as the two standalone helpers below;
204	Stage~6 (the remaining Stacks~00OE smooth-algebra dimension formula
205	plus Stacks~02JK cotangent--K\"ahler-over-a-field bridge) is the open
206	Mathlib gap, see the \texttt{\% NOTE (iter-194)} block on
207	\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} just below.
208	
209	\begin{lemma}
210	  [Stage 5a --- K\"ahler-differentials freeness transports through localisation]
211	  \label{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.module_free_kaehlerDifferential_localization}
213	  Let \(R\) and \(S\) be commutative rings equipped with an \(R\)-algebra
214	  structure on \(S\), and assume that the module of K\"ahler differentials
215	  \(\Omega_{S/R}\) is free as an \(S\)-module. Let \(M \subseteq S\) be a
216	  submonoid and let \(S_M\) be a localisation of \(S\) at \(M\), so that
217	  \(R \to S \to S_M\) is a tower of \(R\)-algebras. Then the module of
218	  K\"ahler differentials \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) is free as an
219	  \(S_M\)-module.
220	\end{lemma}
221	
222	\begin{proof}
223	  The canonical comparison map
224	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) induced by the localisation map
225	  \(S \to S_M\) realises \(\Omega_{S_M/R}\) as the localisation of the
226	  \(S\)-module \(\Omega_{S/R}\) at \(M\); i.e.\ it satisfies Mathlib's
227	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} predicate along the underlying ring
228	  localisation. This is Stacks tag \texttt{02JK}, packaged in Mathlib as
229	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map}. Freeness of a
230	  module is preserved under localisation of modules via Mathlib's
231	  \texttt{Module.free\_of\_isLocalizedModule}; combining the two yields
232	  the conclusion.
233	\end{proof}
234	
235	\begin{lemma}
236	  [Stage 5b --- K\"ahler-differentials rank under localisation equals
237	  the relative dimension]
238	  \label{lem:rank_kaehler_localization_eq_relative_dim}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.rank_kaehlerDifferential_localization_eq_relativeDimension}
240	  \uses{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}
241	  With notation as in \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization},
242	  assume in addition that the \(R\)-algebra \(S\) is \emph{standard
243	  smooth of relative dimension \(n\)} (Mathlib's
244	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,\(n\)\,\(R\)\,\(S\),
245	  the algebra-side packaging of Stacks tag \texttt{00T7}(2)) and that
246	  the localisation \(S_M\) is non-trivial as a ring. Then the rank of
247	  \(\Omega_{S_M / R}\) over \(S_M\) equals \(n\).
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  Mathlib's
252	  \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.\linebreak rank\_kaehlerDifferential}
253	  asserts \(\mathrm{rank}_S(\Omega_{S/R}) = n\) under the
254	  standard-smooth hypothesis. The localisation map
255	  \(\Omega_{S/R} \to \Omega_{S_M/R}\) is an
256	  \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} along \(S \to S_M\) (Stacks tag
257	  \texttt{02JK}; cf.\ the proof of
258	  \cref{lem:module_free_kaehler_localization}), and Mathlib's
259	  \texttt{Module.lift\_rank\_of\_isLocalizedModule\_of\_free} transports
260	  module rank through an \texttt{IsLocalizedModule} up to
261	  \(\mathrm{Cardinal.lift}\). Since \(n\) is a natural number both
262	  ranks equal \(n\), so
263	  \(\mathrm{rank}_{S_M}(\Omega_{S_M / R}) = n\) as required.
264	\end{proof}

397	  % `Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`, see

410	  Hartshorne~I.5.1 states classically). Localising a regular local ring at a
411	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
412	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
413	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a

415	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
416	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the

433	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
434	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
435	
436	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
437	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
438	point.
439	
440	\begin{theorem}
441	
442	\leanok
443	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
444	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
445	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
446	  
447	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
448	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
449	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
450	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
451	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
452	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
453	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
454	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
455	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
456	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
457	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
458	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
459	
460	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
461	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
462	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
463	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
464	  in \(X\).
465	\end{theorem}
466	
467	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
468	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
469	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
470	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
471	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
472	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
473	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
474	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
475	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
476	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
477	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
478	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
479	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
480	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
481	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
482	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
483	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
484	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
485	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
486	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
487	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
488	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
489	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
490	% which is a contradiction."
491	\begin{proof}
492	  
493	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
494	
495	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
496	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
497	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
498	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
499	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
500	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
501	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
502	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
503	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
504	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
505	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
506	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
507	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
508	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
509	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
510	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
511	
512	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
513	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
514	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
515	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
516	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
517	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
518	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
519	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
520	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
521	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
522	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
523	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
524	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
525	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
526	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
527	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
528	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
529	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
530	
531	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
532	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
533	  agree on all of \(X\).
534	\end{proof}
535	
536	\begin{remark}
537	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
538	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
539	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
540	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
541	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
542	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
543	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
544	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
545	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
546	
547	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
548	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
549	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
550	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
551	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
552	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
553	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
554	\end{remark}
555	
556	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
557	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
558	
559	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
560	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
561	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
562	this forces the locus to be empty.
563	
564	\begin{lemma}
565	
566	\leanok
567	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
568	  variety]
569	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
570	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
571	  
572	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
573	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
574	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
575	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
576	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
577	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
578	  % divisors).}"
579	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
580	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
581	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
582	  G\( be a rational map. Then either \)f\( is defined on all of \)X$, or the indeterminacy
583	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
584	  union of prime divisors).
585	
586	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
587	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
588	\end{lemma}
589	
590	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Define a rational map
591	%   \(\Phi \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow G\), $\;(x, y) \mapsto \varphi(x) \cdot
592	%   \varphi(y)^{-1}$.
593	% More precisely, if \((U, \varphi_U)\) represents \(\varphi\), then \(\Phi\) is the rational
594	% map represented by
595	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G
596	%   \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}} G
597	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
598	% Clearly \(\Phi\) is defined at a diagonal point \((x, x)\) if \(\varphi\) is defined at
599	% \(x\), and then \(\Phi(x, x) = e\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\), then
600	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of \((x, x)\); in particular, there will be an
601	% open subset \(U\) of \(V\) such that \(\Phi\) is defined on \(\{x\} \times U\). After
602	% possible replacing \(U\) by a smaller open subset (not necessarily containing \(x\)),
603	% \(\varphi\) will be defined on \(U\). For \(u \in U\), the formula
604	%   \(\varphi(x) = \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u)\)
605	% defines \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Thus \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\) if and only if \(\Phi\) is
606	% defined at \((x, x)\). The rational map \(\Phi\) defines a map
607	%   \(\Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \to k(V \times V)\).
608	% Since \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) if it is defined there, it follows that \(\Phi\)
609	% is defined at \((x, x)\) if and only if
610	%   \(\operatorname{Im}(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subset \mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)}\).
611	% Now \(V \times V\) is nonsingular, and so we have a good theory of divisors (AG,
612	% Chapter 12). For a nonzero rational function \(f\) on \(V \times V\), write
613	%   \(\operatorname{div}(f) = \operatorname{div}(f)_0 - \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\),
614	% with \(\operatorname{div}(f)_0\) and \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\) effective divisors
615	% --- note that \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty = \operatorname{div}(f^{-1})_0\). Then
616	%   $\mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)} = \{f \in k(V \times V) \mid
617	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
618	% Suppose \(\phi\) is not defined at \(x\). Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
619	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
620	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
621	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
622	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
623	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
624	\begin{proof}
625	  
626	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
627	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
628	
629	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
630	  \[
631	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
632	    \qquad
633	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
634	  \]
635	  via the composition
636	  \[
637	    U \times U
638	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
639	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
640	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
641	  \]
642	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
643	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
644	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
645	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
646	
647	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
648	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
649	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
650	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
651	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
652	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
653	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
654	  identity
655	  \[
656	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
657	    \qquad u \in W',
658	  \]
659	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
660	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
661	
662	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
663	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
664	  \(K(X \times X)\):
665	  \[
666	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
667	  \]
668	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
669	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
670	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
671	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
672	
673	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
674	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
675	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
676	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
677	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
678	  \[
679	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
680	  \]
681	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
682	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
683	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
684	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
685	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
686	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
687	  \[
688	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
689	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
690	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
691	  \]
692	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
693	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
694	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
695	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
696	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
697	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
698	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
699	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
700	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
701	
702	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
703	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
704	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
705	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
706	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
707	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
708	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
709	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
710	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
711	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
712	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
713	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
714	\end{proof}
715	
716	\begin{remark}
717	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
718	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
719	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
720	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
721	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
722	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
723	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
724	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
725	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
726	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
727	\end{remark}

729	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
730	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
731	
732	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
733	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
734	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
735	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
736	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
737	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
738	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
739	
740	\begin{theorem}
741	
742	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
743	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
744	  
745	  % NOTE (iter-179 review, lvb-checker `codimone-iter179` corrective):

774	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation \(v_Y\) associated to a
775	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}\) as the
776	  % subring of \(K(X)\) on which \(v_Y \geq 0\)) (read from
777	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
778	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
779	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
780	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\). We call the
781	  % corresponding discrete valuation \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
782	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function on \(X\).
783	  % Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has a \emph{zero} along
784	  % \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\) has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of
785	  % order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
786	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map).}
787	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
788	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
789	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
790	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
791	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
792	  \begin{enumerate}
793	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
794	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
795	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
796	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
797	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
798	      satisfying
799	      \[
800	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
801	      \]
802	  \end{enumerate}
803	
804	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
805	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
806	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
807	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
808	\end{theorem}
809	
810	\begin{proof}
811	  
812	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
813	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
814	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
815	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
816	  \[
817	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
818	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
819	  \]
820	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
821	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
822	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
823	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
824	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
825	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
826	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
827	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
828	
829	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
830	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
831	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
832	\end{proof}
833	
834	\begin{remark}
835	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
836	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
837	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
838	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
839	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
840	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
841	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
842	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
843	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
844	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
845	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
846	\end{remark}
847	
848	\begin{lemma}
849	
850	\leanok
851	  [Definedness at a prime divisor, repackaged as a through-the-point partial map]
852	  \label{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle}
853	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point}
854	  Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be varieties over \(\bar k\) and let
855	  \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. For a prime divisor
856	  \(W \subseteq X\) with generic point \(\eta_W = W.\mathrm{point}\), the
857	  following are equivalent:
858	  \begin{enumerate}
859	    \item \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) (\(f\) is defined at the generic point of
860	      \(W\)).
861	    \item There exists a partial-map representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\) ---
862	      i.e.\ a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) on a dense open
863	      \(U \subseteq X\) whose induced rational map equals \(f\) --- whose domain
864	      \(U\) contains \(\eta_W\).
865	  \end{enumerate}
866	\end{lemma}
867	
868	\begin{proof}
869	
870	  The lemma is a definitional reshuffle of the Mathlib membership predicate
871	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.mem\_domain}, which already states that
872	  \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) iff there is some partial-map representative of
873	  \(f\) defined at \(\eta_W\); the only content here is reordering the two
874	  conjuncts under the existential so the \(\varphi_U \mapsto f\) component lands
875	  first (matching the way later witnesses in the project construct partial-map
876	  representatives). The forward and backward implications are immediate from
877	  the unfolded definition.
878	\end{proof}
879	
880	\begin{remark}
881	  \label{rmk:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle_scope}
882	  \textbf{Relationship to \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}.}
883	  \cref{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} is the iter-179 Lane~D honest
884	  fallback for the substantive Hartshorne-II.6 ``regular = no pole''
885	  reformulation that \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} states in its full
886	  \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form. The reshuffle captures only the
887	  definitional half of the equivalence (existence of a partial-map
888	  representative through a prime-divisor generic point); upgrading it to the
889	  substantive \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form requires the
890	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap}-to-function-field pullback machinery, which is
891	  not shipped by Mathlib at commit \texttt{b80f227}. See the iter-179
892	  \texttt{\% NOTE} block on \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} for the
893	  history.
894	\end{remark}

1008	\section{Out of scope}
1009	\label{sec:codim1_out_of_scope}
1010	
1011	This chapter packages \emph{only} the two extension inputs and the Weil-divisor
1012	reformulation needed for A.4.a. The following adjacent content is explicitly out of
1013	scope:
1014	\begin{itemize}
1015	  \item \textbf{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the regular \(\Rightarrow\)
1016	    Cohen--Macaulay corollary.} Proved in \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
1017	    as \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} and \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}; this
1018	    chapter only consumes them (in Step~2 of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}; see
1019	    \cref{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}).
1020	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.2 itself.} The combination of
1021	    \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} into the
1022	    statement ``a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
1023	    extends'' lives in \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} as
1024	    \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}; this chapter only supplies the two inputs.
1025	  \item \textbf{Milne Theorem~3.4 / Corollary~3.7 / Theorem~3.8 / Proposition~3.9 /
1026	    Proposition~3.10.} The remainder of Milne~\S I.3 (the ``map between AVs is
1027	    translated homomorphism'' family of results, and the
1028	    \(\mathbb A^1 \dashrightarrow A\) / unirational\(\dashrightarrow A\) constancy
1029	    statements) is downstream of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} but is not
1030	    needed by A.4 in the project's current routing: the genus-\(0\) base case is
1031	    handled via the proven Rigidity Lemma + \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut
1032	    (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), and the positive-genus arm consumes only the bare
1033	    Theorem~3.2 statement.
1034	  \item \textbf{Weil-divisor degree on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve.} The proof of
1035	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} invokes the Weil-divisor apparatus on the
1036	    smooth surface \(X \times X\) but does \emph{not} need the degree map of
1037	    \cref{def:divisor_degree} (which is curve-specific); only the codim-\(1\)
1038	    decomposition \(\div(f) = \div(f)_0 - \div(f)_\infty\) on a nonsingular surface is
1039	    used. The bilinear degree formula on \(X \times X\) for \(X\) a curve (intersection
1040	    numbers, Hartshorne~V.1) is out of scope.
1041	  \item \textbf{Cartier divisors and the Cartier/Weil comparison.} On a smooth variety
1042	    they coincide and the chapter uses Weil throughout; the Cartier formalism is not
1043	    introduced.
1044	  \item \textbf{Generalisation to non-algebraically-closed base fields.} The
1045	    project's positive-genus arm runs over \(\bar k\) throughout; descent of the
1046	    extension to a non-algebraically-closed base, if ever needed, would require
1047	    Galois-descent machinery and is not in scope.
1048	  \item \textbf{Resolution of indeterminacy by blow-ups} (Hironaka-style, for higher
1049	    indeterminacy codimensions on a smooth variety with a non-group-variety target):
1050	    out of scope. The combination of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} and
1051	    \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} for \(G\) a group variety is sharper than any
1052	    blow-up-based approach because it produces an extension on the original \(X\)
1053	    rather than on a blow-up; no blow-up apparatus is needed.
1054	\end{itemize}

1	/-
2	Copyright (c) 2025 Nailin Guan. All rights reserved.
3	Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
4	Authors: Nailin Guan
5	-/
6	module
7	
8	public import Mathlib.Algebra.Module.SpanRankOperations
9	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.DiscreteValuationRing.Basic
10	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.KrullsHeightTheorem
11	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.Field
12	public import Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.PID
13	
14	/-!
15	# Regular local rings
16	
17	For a Noetherian local ring `R`, we define `IsRegularLocalRing` as
18	`(maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = ringKrullDim R`. This definition is equivalent to
19	the dimension of the cotangent space over the residue field being equal to `ringKrullDim R`,
20	(see `IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace`).
21	
22	# Main Definition and Results
23	
24	* `IsRegularLocalRing` : A Noetherian local ring is regular if
25	  `(maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = ringKrullDim R`,
26	  i.e. its maximal ideal can be generated by `dim R` elements.
27	
28	* `IsRegularLocalRing.iff_finrank_cotangentSpace` : the equivalence of `IsRegularLocalRing` and
29	  `Module.finrank (ResidueField R) (CotangentSpace R) = ringKrullDim R`
30	
31	-/
32	
33	@[expose] public section
34	
35	open IsLocalRing
36	
37	/-- A Noetherian local ring is said to be regular if its maximal ideal
38	can be generated by `dim R` elements. -/
39	@[stacks 00KU]
40	class IsRegularLocalRing (R : Type*) [CommRing R] : Prop extends
41	    IsLocalRing R, IsNoetherianRing R where
42	  spanFinrank_maximalIdeal : (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = ringKrullDim R
43	
44	variable (R : Type*) [CommRing R]
45	
46	lemma isRegularLocalRing_iff [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R] :
47	    IsRegularLocalRing R ↔ (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = ringKrullDim R :=
48	  ⟨fun ⟨h⟩ ↦ h, fun h ↦ ⟨h⟩⟩
49	
50	namespace IsRegularLocalRing
51	
52	lemma of_spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_le [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
53	    (le : (maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank ≤ ringKrullDim R) : IsRegularLocalRing R :=
54	  (isRegularLocalRing_iff _).mpr (le_antisymm le (ringKrullDim_le_spanFinrank_maximalIdeal R))
55	
56	variable {R} in
57	lemma of_ringEquiv [IsRegularLocalRing R] {R' : Type*} [CommRing R']
58	    (e : R ≃+* R') : IsRegularLocalRing R' := by
59	  have := e.isLocalRing
60	  have := isNoetherianRing_of_ringEquiv R e
61	  rwa [isRegularLocalRing_iff, ← ringKrullDim_eq_of_ringEquiv e, ← map_ringEquiv_maximalIdeal e,
62	    Ideal.spanFinrank_map_eq_of_ringEquiv, ← isRegularLocalRing_iff]
63	
64	lemma iff_finrank_cotangentSpace [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R] :
65	    IsRegularLocalRing R ↔ Module.finrank (ResidueField R) (CotangentSpace R) = ringKrullDim R := by
66	  rw [isRegularLocalRing_iff, spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_eq_finrank_cotangentSpace]
67	
68	instance [IsLocalRing R] [IsDomain R] [IsPrincipalIdealRing R] : IsRegularLocalRing R := by
69	  by_cases isf : IsField R
70	  · let _ := isf.toField
71	    simp [isRegularLocalRing_iff, maximalIdeal_eq_bot]
72	  · apply of_spanFinrank_maximalIdeal_le
73	    rcases IsPrincipalIdealRing.principal (maximalIdeal R) with ⟨x, hx⟩
74	    simpa only [(IsPrincipalIdealRing.ringKrullDim_eq_one R) isf,
75	      Nat.cast_le_one, ← Set.ncard_singleton x, hx] using
76	        Submodule.spanFinrank_span_le_ncard_of_finite (Set.finite_singleton x)
77	
78	end IsRegularLocalRing

503	def RationalMap.openCoverDomain (f : X ⤏ Y) : f.domain.toScheme.OpenCover where
504	  I₀ := { PartialMap.domain g | (g) (_ : g.toRationalMap = f) }
505	  X U := U.1.toScheme
506	  f U := X.homOfLE (le_sSup U.2)
507	  mem₀ := by
508	    rw [presieve₀_mem_precoverage_iff]
509	    refine ⟨fun x ↦ ?_, inferInstance⟩
510	    use ⟨_, (TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_sSup.mp x.2).choose_spec.1⟩
511	    exact ⟨⟨x.1, (TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_sSup.mp x.2).choose_spec.2⟩, Subtype.ext (by simp)⟩

514	/-- If `f : X ⤏ Y` is a rational map from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme,
515	then `f` can be represented as a partial map on its domain of definition. -/
516	noncomputable
517	def RationalMap.toPartialMap [IsReduced X] [Y.IsSeparated] (f : X ⤏ Y) : X.PartialMap Y := by
518	  refine ⟨f.domain, f.dense_domain, f.openCoverDomain.glueMorphisms
519	    (fun x ↦ (X.isoOfEq x.2.choose_spec.2).inv ≫ x.2.choose.hom) ?_⟩
520	  intro x y
521	  let g (x : f.openCoverDomain.I₀) := x.2.choose
522	  have hg₁ (x) : (g x).toRationalMap = f := x.2.choose_spec.1
523	  have hg₂ (x) : (g x).domain = x.1 := x.2.choose_spec.2
524	  refine (cancel_epi (isPullback_opens_inf_le (le_sSup x.2) (le_sSup y.2)).isoPullback.hom).mp ?_
525	  simp only [openCoverDomain, IsPullback.isoPullback_hom_fst_assoc,
526	    IsPullback.isoPullback_hom_snd_assoc]
527	  change _ ≫ _ ≫ (g x).hom = _ ≫ _ ≫ (g y).hom
528	  simp_rw [← cancel_epi (X.isoOfEq congr($(hg₂ x) ⊓ $(hg₂ y))).hom, ← Category.assoc]
529	  convert (PartialMap.equiv_iff_of_isSeparated (S := ⊤_ _) (f := g x) (g := g y)).mp ?_ using 1
530	  · dsimp; congr 1; simp [g, ← cancel_mono (Opens.ι _)]
531	  · dsimp; congr 1; simp [g, ← cancel_mono (Opens.ι _)]
532	  · rw [← PartialMap.toRationalMap_eq_iff, hg₁, hg₁]
533

535	    (f : X.PartialMap Y) : (f.toRationalMap.toPartialMap.restrict _ f.dense_domain
536	      f.le_domain_toRationalMap).hom = f.hom := by
537	  dsimp [RationalMap.toPartialMap]
538	  refine (f.toRationalMap.openCoverDomain.ι_glueMorphisms _ _ ⟨_, f, rfl, rfl⟩).trans ?_
539	  generalize_proofs _ _ H _
540	  have : H.choose = f := (equiv_iff_of_domain_eq_of_isSeparated (S := ⊤_ _) H.choose_spec.2).mp
541	    (toRationalMap_eq_iff.mp H.choose_spec.1)
542	  exact ((ext_iff _ _).mp this.symm).choose_spec.symm

547	  obtain ⟨f, rfl⟩ := PartialMap.toRationalMap_surjective f
548	  trans (f.toRationalMap.toPartialMap.restrict _

552	    exact PartialMap.ext _ f rfl (by simpa using f.toPartialMap_toRationalMap_restrict)

556	  rw [← PartialMap.isOver_toRationalMap_iff_of_isSeparated, f.toRationalMap_toPartialMap]

Explanation: The rewrite tactic rewrites an expression 'e' using an equality 'a = b' by the following process. First, it looks for all 'a' in 'e'. Second, it tries to abstract these occurrences of 'a' to create a function 'm := fun _a => ...', called the *motive*, with the property that 'm a' is definitionally equal to 'e'. Third, we observe that 'congrArg' implies that 'm a = m b', which can be used with lemmas such as 'Eq.mpr' to change the goal. However, if 'e' depends on specific properties of 'a', then the motive 'm' might not typecheck.

Possible solutions: use rewrite's 'occs' configuration option to limit which occurrences are rewritten, or use 'simp' or 'conv' mode, which have strategies for certain kinds of dependencies (these tactics can handle proofs and 'Decidable' instances whose types depend on the rewritten term, and 'simp' can apply user-defined '@[congr]' theorems as well).

content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).

42	/-! ### Recipe 1 helpers (iter-184 Lane B, `analogies/gmscaling-projection-idiom.md`):
43	project-side simp helpers for `pullback.map ≫ pullback.fst/snd`.
44	
45	Mathlib's `pullback.lift_fst` / `pullback.lift_snd` are `@[reassoc]` ONLY, not
46	`@[simp]`, so the abbrev `pullback.map _ _ _ _ i₁ i₂ i₃ _ _ ≫ pullback.fst _ _`
47	is not collapsed by `simp` even though the equation holds definitionally. These
48	two local simp lemmas unblock projection-chain collapse for
49	`gmScalingP1_cover_intersection_X_iso` whose STEP 4 uses
50	`asIso (pullback.map …)`. Candidate for upstream Mathlib contribution. -/
51	
52	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
53	lemma pullback_map_fst_proj {C : Type*} [Category C] {W X Y Z S T : C}
54	    (f₁ : W ⟶ S) (f₂ : X ⟶ S) [Limits.HasPullback f₁ f₂] (g₁ : Y ⟶ T)
55	    (g₂ : Z ⟶ T) [Limits.HasPullback g₁ g₂] (i₁ : W ⟶ Y) (i₂ : X ⟶ Z) (i₃ : S ⟶ T)
56	    (eq₁ : f₁ ≫ i₃ = i₁ ≫ g₁) (eq₂ : f₂ ≫ i₃ = i₂ ≫ g₂) :
57	    Limits.pullback.map f₁ f₂ g₁ g₂ i₁ i₂ i₃ eq₁ eq₂ ≫
58	        Limits.pullback.fst g₁ g₂ =
59	      Limits.pullback.fst f₁ f₂ ≫ i₁ :=
60	  Limits.pullback.lift_fst _ _ _
61	
62	@[reassoc (attr := simp)]
63	lemma pullback_map_snd_proj {C : Type*} [Category C] {W X Y Z S T : C}
64	    (f₁ : W ⟶ S) (f₂ : X ⟶ S) [Limits.HasPullback f₁ f₂] (g₁ : Y ⟶ T)
65	    (g₂ : Z ⟶ T) [Limits.HasPullback g₁ g₂] (i₁ : W ⟶ Y) (i₂ : X ⟶ Z) (i₃ : S ⟶ T)
66	    (eq₁ : f₁ ≫ i₃ = i₁ ≫ g₁) (eq₂ : f₂ ≫ i₃ = i₂ ≫ g₂) :
67	    Limits.pullback.map f₁ f₂ g₁ g₂ i₁ i₂ i₃ eq₁ eq₂ ≫
68	        Limits.pullback.snd g₁ g₂ =
69	      Limits.pullback.snd f₁ f₂ ≫ i₂ :=
70	  Limits.pullback.lift_snd _ _ _
71	
72	/-! ### Chart-bridge: `Proj.awayι ≫ PLB.hom = Spec.map (algebraMap kbar (Away _ _))`
73	
74	The helper below is the iter-173 `mathlib-analogist chart-bridge173` recipe step (a)
75	(`analogies/chart-bridge.md`). Used by `gmScalingP1_cover_X_iso` (below the `gmScalingP1_cover`
76	definition). -/
77	
78	/-- **`Proj.awayι 𝒜 f _ _ ≫ PLB.hom = Spec.map (algebraMap kbar (Away 𝒜 f))`** for any
79	homogeneous element `f` of positive degree.
80	
81	Generic in the element `f` AND in the degree `m` so we can apply it to either
82	`(![X 0, X 1]) i` (the actual chart input from `projectiveLineBarAffineCover.openCover.f i`,
83	which uses `m = (![1, 1]) i`) or `X i` (which uses `m = 1`). A pure rewrite chasing
84	`awayι_toSpecZero` + `Spec.map_comp` + the `algebraKbarAway` defeq. -/
85	private lemma awayι_comp_PLB_hom (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar]
86	    {m : ℕ} (hm : 0 < m)
87	    (f : MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) (hf : f ∈ projectiveLineBarGrading kbar m) :
88	    Proj.awayι (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar) f hf hm ≫
89	      (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom =
90	    Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap kbar
91	      (HomogeneousLocalization.Away (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar) f))) := by
92	  change Proj.awayι _ _ _ _ ≫ Proj.toSpecZero _ ≫ Spec.map _ = _
93	  rw [← Category.assoc, Proj.awayι_toSpecZero, ← Spec.map_comp,
94	    ← CommRingCat.ofHom_comp]
95	  rfl
96	
97	/-- **`ℙ¹` is reduced.** Closed axiom-clean iter-168 via `IsReduced.of_openCover` over
98	`projectiveLineBarAffineCover`; each chart `Spec (HomogeneousLocalization.Away 𝒜 (X_i))`
99	is a domain because the canonical `val`-injection into `Localization.Away (X_i)` (a
100	localization of `k̄[X_0, X_1]` at a non-zero-divisor, hence a domain) factors through
101	`Function.Injective.isDomain`. Exported here for Lane B (replaces its inline `haveI hP1red`).
102	
103	**Moved to this location iter-193 Lane B**: previously declared after the
104	`gmScalingP1_*` block; relocated so the cocycle proof
105	`gmScalingP1_chart_agreement_cross01` can pick up the
106	`IsReduced (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` instance needed by Substrate 1
107	(`IsClosedImmersion.lift_iff_range_subset`). -/
108	instance projectiveLineBar_isReduced (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] :
109	    IsReduced (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left := by
110	  change IsReduced (ProjectiveLineBarScheme kbar)
111	  -- Strategy: `IsReduced.of_openCover` over `projectiveLineBarAffineCover.openCover`.
112	  -- Each chart is `Spec(.of (Away 𝒜 (X i)))`; `IsReduced (Spec R)` if `R` is reduced.
113	  -- `Away 𝒜 (X i)` is a domain (and hence reduced) because it embeds via `val_injective`
114	  -- into `Localization.Away (X i)`, which is a localization of `MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar`
115	  -- (a domain) at a non-zero-divisor — hence a domain.
116	  haveI : ∀ i : Fin 2, IsReduced ((projectiveLineBarAffineCover kbar).openCover.X i) := by
117	    intro i
118	    change IsReduced (Spec (CommRingCat.of (HomogeneousLocalization.Away
119	        (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar) ((![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] :
120	          Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) i))))
121	    haveI : IsDomain (Localization.Away ((![(MvPolynomial.X 0 : MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar),
122	        MvPolynomial.X 1] : Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) i)) := by
123	      fin_cases i <;>
124	        exact IsLocalization.isDomain_localization
125	          (powers_le_nonZeroDivisors_of_noZeroDivisors (MvPolynomial.X_ne_zero _))
126	    haveI : IsDomain (HomogeneousLocalization.Away (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar)
127	        ((![(MvPolynomial.X 0 : MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar), MvPolynomial.X 1] :
128	          Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) i)) := by
129	      refine Function.Injective.isDomain
130	        (algebraMap
131	          (HomogeneousLocalization.Away (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar)
132	            ((![(MvPolynomial.X 0 : MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar), MvPolynomial.X 1] :
133	              Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) i))
134	          (Localization.Away
135	            ((![(MvPolynomial.X 0 : MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar), MvPolynomial.X 1] :
136	              Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) i))) ?_
137	      intro x y h
138	      exact HomogeneousLocalization.val_injective _ h
139	    infer_instance
140	  exact IsReduced.of_openCover _ (projectiveLineBarAffineCover kbar).openCover
141	
142	/-! ### (D) The `𝔾_m`-scaling action `σ_× : ℙ¹ × 𝔾_m ⟶ ℙ¹`
143	
144	`gmScalingP1` is a *bare* `Over (Spec (.of kbar))`-morphism (the analogist D3 verdict:
145	no `IsAction`/`MulAction`-style typeclass at scheme level — Mathlib has no such precedent;
146	the rigidity consumer needs only the bare morphism + the named fixed-point lemma).
147	
148	Chartwise definition: on `𝔸¹ × 𝔾_m` (target chart `D₊(X₀)` of `ℙ¹`), the morphism is
149	the polynomial map `(x, λ) ↦ λx`; near `∞` (target chart `D₊(X₁)`, coordinate `u = 1/x`),
150	the target coordinate `1/(λx) = u/λ` is regular because `λ ∈ 𝔾_m` is invertible. The two
151	chart-restrictions agree on `(𝔸¹ ∖ {0}) × 𝔾_m`, so they glue via
152	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms`.
153	
154	The companion lemma `gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero` exposes the load-bearing fixed-point
155	property `σ_×(0, λ) = 0` for all `λ ∈ 𝔾_m`, packaged as the `W`-axis-collapse hypothesis
156	that `hom_additive_decomp_of_rigidity` (Cor 1.5) consumes. -/
157	
158	/-- **Chart-1 ring map for `σ_×`** at the `MvPolynomial Unit kbar`-level: sends the affine
159	coord `u = X 0 / X 1 ↦ u ⊗ λ`, where `λ = X () ∈ GmRing kbar`. Uses
160	`MvPolynomial.eval₂Hom` with the algebra-map `kbar →+* MvPolynomial Unit kbar ⊗[kbar] GmRing`
161	(target carrier carries `Algebra kbar` because both factors do). Axiom-clean. -/
162	noncomputable def gmScalingP1_chart1_ringMap (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] :
163	    MvPolynomial Unit kbar →+* TensorProduct kbar (MvPolynomial Unit kbar) (GmRing kbar) :=
164	  MvPolynomial.eval₂Hom (algebraMap kbar _)
165	    (fun _ => (MvPolynomial.X () : MvPolynomial Unit kbar) ⊗ₜ[kbar]
166	      (algebraMap (MvPolynomial Unit kbar) (GmRing kbar) (MvPolynomial.X ())))
167	
168	/-- **Chart-0 ring map for `σ_×`** at the `MvPolynomial Unit kbar`-level: sends the affine
169	coord `t = X 1 / X 0 ↦ t ⊗ λ⁻¹`. The `λ⁻¹` is `IsLocalization.Away.invSelf (X ())` in
170	`GmRing kbar = Localization.Away (X () : MvPolynomial Unit kbar)`. Axiom-clean. -/
171	noncomputable def gmScalingP1_chart0_ringMap (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] :
172	    MvPolynomial Unit kbar →+* TensorProduct kbar (MvPolynomial Unit kbar) (GmRing kbar) :=
173	  MvPolynomial.eval₂Hom (algebraMap kbar _)
174	    (fun _ => (MvPolynomial.X () : MvPolynomial Unit kbar) ⊗ₜ[kbar]
175	      (IsLocalization.Away.invSelf
176	        (MvPolynomial.X () : MvPolynomial Unit kbar) :
177	        GmRing kbar))
178	
179	/-- **The pullback open cover of `(ℙ¹ ⊗ 𝔾_m).left`** along `pullback.fst`, indexed by
180	the 2-chart cover `projectiveLineBarAffineCover` of `ProjectiveLineBar.left`. The `i`-th
181	component is `pullback (pullback.fst PLB.hom Gm.hom) (Proj.awayι 𝒜 (X i) …)`. -/
182	noncomputable def gmScalingP1_cover (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] :
183	    ((ProjectiveLineBar kbar) ⊗ Gm kbar).left.OpenCover :=
184	  (projectiveLineBarAffineCover kbar).openCover.pullback₁
185	    (pullback.fst (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).hom (Gm kbar).hom)
186	
187	/-- **The chart-`i` source of `gmScalingP1_cover` is
188	`Spec ((Away 𝒜 ((![X 0, X 1]) i)) ⊗[kbar] GmRing kbar)`.**
189	
190	Built by composing `pullbackSymmetry`, `pullbackRightPullbackFstIso`, the
191	`awayι_comp_PLB_hom` rewrite via `pullback.congrHom`, and `pullbackSpecIso`. Mirrors the
192	Mathlib precedent `OpenCover.pullbackCoverAffineRefinementObjIso`
193	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open:160-166`). Used by `gmScalingP1_chart`.
194	
195	**Uniform-in-`i` (iter-179 refactor `cover-bridge-uniform-i`):** the target type carries
196	`((![X 0, X 1]) i)` rather than `MvPolynomial.X i`. The hoisted helpers
197	`projectiveLineBarAffineCover_fDeg`/`_hm` together with the m-generalised
198	`awayι_comp_PLB_hom` keep the bridge chain syntactic so `pullbackSpecIso` applies
199	generically — no `match`-on-`i` decoration. -/
200	private noncomputable def gmScalingP1_cover_X_iso (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] (i : Fin 2) :
201	    (gmScalingP1_cover kbar).X i ≅
202	      Spec (CommRingCat.of
203	        (TensorProduct kbar
204	          (HomogeneousLocalization.Away (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar)
205	            ((![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] :
206	              Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) i))
207	          (GmRing kbar))) :=
208	  pullbackSymmetry _ _ ≪≫
209	    pullbackRightPullbackFstIso _ _ _ ≪≫
210	    pullback.congrHom
211	      (awayι_comp_PLB_hom kbar (projectiveLineBarAffineCover_hm i)
212	        ((![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] :
213	          Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) i)
214	        (projectiveLineBarAffineCover_fDeg kbar i))
215	      (show (Gm kbar).hom =
216	          Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap kbar (GmRing kbar))) from rfl) ≪≫
217	    pullbackSpecIso kbar _ (GmRing kbar)
218	
219	/-- **The chart-`i` scheme morphism** `(gmScalingP1_cover kbar).X i ⟶ ProjectiveLineBarScheme`
220	defining `σ_×` on the `i`-th chart. On chart-1 (target `D₊(X 1)`), the affine coord
221	`u = X 0 / X 1` is sent to `u ⊗ λ`; on chart-0 (target `D₊(X 0)`), `t = X 1 / X 0` is sent
222	to `t ⊗ λ⁻¹`. The scheme map is built from `gmScalingP1_chart{0,1}_ringMap` (the chart-side
223	ring maps) via `pullbackSpecIso` + (the chart-ring iso
224	`HomogeneousLocalization.Away ≃+* MvPolynomial Unit kbar`) + `Proj.awayι`.
225
