466	\section{Sub-lemmas of the construction}
467	\label{sec:quot_sub_lemmas}
468	
469	\begin{lemma}[Reduction to the universal case]
470	  \label{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W}
471	  
472	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Reduction to the case of
473	  % \(\Quot_{\pi^*W/\PP(V)/S}^{\Phi,L}\)"
474	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
475	  % L2294-L2332).
476	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
477	  % "It is enough to prove Theorem REF in the special case
478	  %  that \(X = \PP(V)\) and
479	  %  \(E= \pi^*(W)\) where \(V\) and \(W\) are vector bundles on \(S\), as
480	  %  a consequence of the next lemma.
481	  %
482	  %  \begin{lemma}
483	  %  (i) Let \(\nu\) be any integer.
484	  %  Then tensoring by \(L^{\nu}\) gives an isomorphism of
485	  %  functors from \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) to
486	  %  \(\Quot_{E(\nu)/X/S}^{\Psi,L}\) where the polynomial
487	  %  \(\Psi \in \Q[\lambda]\) is defined by \(\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)\).
488	  %
489	  %  (ii) Let \(\phi : E \to G\) be a surjective homomorphism of
490	  %  coherent sheaves on \(X\). Then the corresponding natural transformation
491	  %  \(\Quot_{G/X/S}^{\Phi,L} \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\)
492	  %  is a closed embedding.
493	  %  \end{lemma}"
494	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
495	  Let \(S\), \(X\), \(L\), \(E\), \(\Phi\) be as in \cref{def:quot_functor}.
496	  \begin{enumerate}
497	    \item (Twist-shift.) For \(\nu \in \mathbb{Z}\), tensoring by
498	      \(L^{\otimes \nu}\) gives an isomorphism of functors
499	      $\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \xrightarrow{\sim}
500	      \Quot^{\Psi,L}_{E(\nu)/X/S}\( with \)\Psi(\lambda) = \Phi(\lambda + \nu)$.
501	    \item (Surjection induces closed embedding.) Given a surjection
502	      \(\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G\) of coherent sheaves on \(X\), the
503	      induced natural transformation
504	      \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \to \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) is a closed
505	      embedding of functors.
506	  \end{enumerate}
507	  Consequently, if \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\pi^* W / \mathbb{P}(V)/S}\) is
508	  representable for all \(V\), \(W\) vector bundles on \(S\), then so is
509	  \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) for every \(X \subset \mathbb{P}(V)\) closed and
510	  every coherent \(E\) on \(X\) that is a quotient of \(\pi^*(W)(\nu)|_X\) for
511	  some \(W\) and \(\nu\).
512	\end{lemma}
513	
514	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The statement (i) is obvious. The statement (ii) just
515	% says that given any locally noetherian scheme \(T\) and a family
516	% \(\langle \F,q\rangle \in \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(T)\), there exists
517	% a closed subscheme \(T' \subset T\) with the following universal property:
518	% for any locally noetherian scheme \(U\) and a morphism \(f: U\to T\),
519	% the pulled back homomorphism of \({\mathcal O}_{X_U}\)-modules \(q_U : E_U \to \F_U\)
520	% factors via the pulled back homomorphism \(\phi_U : E_U \to G_U\)
521	% if and only if \(U \to T\) factors via \(T'\hra T\).
522	% This is satisfied by taking \(T'\) to be the vanishing scheme
523	% for the composite homomorphism \(\ker(\phi) \hra  E \stackrel{q}{\to} \F\)
524	% of coherent sheaves on \(X_T\) (see Remark REF),
525	% which makes sense here as both \(\ker(\phi)\) and \(\F\) are coherent
526	% on \(X_T\) and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\)."
527	\begin{proof}
528	  
529	  (i) is direct from the definitions: tensoring with \(L^{\otimes \nu}\) is
530	  an autoequivalence of coherent sheaves on \(X\) (and on every \(X_T\)),
531	  preserving surjectivity, \(T\)-flatness, and properness of support, and
532	  shifting the Hilbert polynomial by \(\nu\) in the variable.
533	
534	  (ii) Given a family \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\) in
535	  \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), the locus where \(q\) factors through
536	  \(\phi : E \twoheadrightarrow G\) is cut out by the vanishing of the
537	  composite $\ker(\phi)|_{X_T} \hookrightarrow E_T \xrightarrow{q}
538	  \mathcal{F}\(. Because \)\mathcal{F}\( is \)T$-flat, the schematic image of
539	  this composite descends to a closed subscheme \(T' \subset T\) such that
540	  \(f : U \to T\) factors through \(T'\) iff \(q_U\) factors through \(\phi_U\).
541	  Hence \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{G/X/S} \hookrightarrow \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\)
542	  is a closed embedding of subfunctors.
543	\end{proof}
544	
545	\begin{lemma}[Uniform Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity bound]
546	  \label{lem:quot_boundedness}
547	  
548	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
549	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
550	  % L2346-L2404). Builds on [Nitsure] \S 2 (Castelnuovo--Mumford regularity)
551	  % and \S 3 (semi-continuity and base-change), cited internally as
552	  % Theorem~"Mumford" and Theorem~"Base-change for flat sheaves".
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
554	  % "We consider the sheaf \(E = \pi^*(W)\) on \(X=\PP(V)\) where
555	  %  \(V\) is a vector bundle on \(S\),
556	  %  and take \(L = {\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)}(1)\).
557	  %  For any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point
558	  %  \(s\) of \(S\), we have an isomorphism
559	  %  \(\PP(V)_s \simeq \PP^n_k\) where \(n=\rank(V)-1\),
560	  %  and the restricted sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is isomorphic to
561	  %  \(\oplus^p{\mathcal O}_{\PP(V)_s}\) where \(p=\rank(W)\).
562	  %  It follows from Theorem REF
563	  %  that given any \(\Phi \in \Q[\lambda]\), there exists an integer
564	  %  \(m\) which depends only on \(\rank(V)\), \(\rank(W)\) and \(\Phi\),
565	  %  such that for any field \(k\) and a \(k\)-valued point \(s\) of \(S\),
566	  %  the sheaf \(E_s\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) is \(m\)-regular, and for any
567	  %  coherent quotient \(q: E_s \to \F\) on \(\PP(V)_s\) with
568	  %  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\), the sheaf \(\F\) and the kernel sheaf
569	  %  \(\G\subset E_s\) of \(q\) are both \(m\)-regular."
570	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity" (FGA Explained
571	  Ch.~5).}
572	  Let \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\) with \(V\), \(W\) vector bundles on
573	  \(S\) of ranks \(n+1\), \(p\), \(L = \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{P}(V)}(1)\), and
574	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). There exists an integer
575	  \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\), depending only on \(n\), \(p\), and \(\Phi\), such that
576	  for every geometric point \(s\) of \(S\) and every coherent quotient
577	  \(q : E_s \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) with Hilbert polynomial
578	  \(\Phi\), the sheaves \(E_s\), \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(\ker q\) on \(X_s\) are all
579	  \(m\)-regular in the sense of Castelnuovo--Mumford. Consequently, for
580	  every \(T \to S\) and every \(T\)-flat quotient
581	  \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) on \(X_T\) with Hilbert
582	  polynomial \(\Phi\) and every \(r \ge m\), the conclusions \textbf{(*)} and
583	  \textbf{(**)} of \cite[\S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"]{nitsure-hilbert-quot}
584	  hold: the direct images \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{F}(r)\), \((\pi_T)_* E_T(r)\),
585	  \((\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}(r)\) are locally free of fixed ranks; the
586	  canonical surjections \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \twoheadrightarrow (-)(r)\)
587	  hold; and the higher direct images \(R^i (\pi_T)_* (-)(r)\) vanish for
588	  \(i \ge 1\).
589	\end{lemma}
590	
591	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see [Nitsure], \S 5, "Use of \(m\)-Regularity"
592	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L2346-L2404).
593	% The proof invokes the m-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2
594	% (Theorem "Mumford") to extract the uniform bound m, and the
595	% base-change-for-flat-sheaves theorem of [Nitsure] \S 3 (cited as
596	% "Base-change for flat sheaves") to propagate the conclusions across \(T\).
597	\begin{proof}
598	  
599	  Each geometric fiber \(E_s\) is a trivial bundle on \(\mathbb{P}^n_{\kappa(s)}\)
600	  of rank \(p\), so \(E_s\) is \(0\)-regular. The Castelnuovo--Mumford
601	  \(m\)-regularity theorem of [Nitsure] \S 2 produces, for any fixed Hilbert
602	  polynomial \(\Phi\), a uniform integer \(m = m(n, p, \Phi)\) above which
603	  every coherent quotient of \(E_s\) with Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi\) -- and
604	  its kernel -- is \(m\)-regular. The Castelnuovo Lemma of [Nitsure] \S 2
605	  converts \(m\)-regularity into vanishing of \(H^i((-) (r))\) for \(i \ge 1\)
606	  and \(r \ge m\), and into global generation of \(H^0((-)(r))\). To
607	  propagate the fiber-wise statement to families over \(T\), apply the
608	  flat-base-change theorem (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}, Stacks
609	  tag 02KH) along \(T \to S\): cohomology of flat families commutes with
610	  base change, so the direct images \((\pi_T)_*(-)(r)\) are locally free of
611	  the rank dictated by the fiber Hilbert polynomials, and the canonical
612	  maps \((\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_*(-)(r) \to (-)(r)\) inherit fiberwise
613	  surjectivity.
614	\end{proof}
615	
616	\begin{lemma}[Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) is injective]
617	  \label{lem:quot_alpha_injective}
618	  
619	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
620	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
621	  % L2409-L2451).
622	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
623	  % "We now fix a positive integer \(r\) such that \(r\ge m\).
624	  %  Note that the rank of \({\pi_T}_*\F(r)\) is \(\Phi(r)\)
625	  %  and \(\pi_*E(r) = W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V\).
626	  %  Therefore the surjective homomorphism
627	  %  \({\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\)
628	  %  defines an element of the set
629	  %  \(\Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V , \Phi(r))(T)\).
630	  %  We thus get a morphism of functors
631	  %  \(\)\alpha : \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \to
632	  %  \Grass( W \otimes_{{\mathcal O}_S}\sym^r V, \Phi(r))\(\)
633	  %  It associates to \(q: E_T \to \F\) the quotient
634	  %  \({\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\).
635	  %
636	  %  The above morphism \(\alpha\) is injective because the quotient
637	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) can be recovered from
638	  %  \({\pi_T}_*(q(r)) : {\pi_T}_*E_T(r) \to {\pi_T}_*\F(r)\)
639	  %  as follows."
640	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Embedding Quot into Grassmannian"
641	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
642	  In the universal setup \(X = \mathbb{P}_S(V)\), \(E = \pi^* W\), fix
643	  \(r \ge m\) from \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}. The natural transformation
644	  \[
645	    \alpha :
646	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} \longrightarrow
647	    \mathrm{Grass}\!\left(
648	      W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V,\; \Phi(r)
649	    \right),
650	    \qquad
651	    \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle \longmapsto
652	    \langle (\pi_T)_*\mathcal{F}(r),\, (\pi_T)_*(q(r))\rangle
653	  \]
654	  is well-defined (the target is locally free of rank \(\Phi(r)\), and the
655	  canonical isomorphism $\pi_* E(r) \cong W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S}
656	  \mathrm{Sym}^r V\( gives the source) and \emph{injective} on \)T$-points:
657	  the data of the locally free quotient at level \(r\) determines
658	  \(q : E_T \to \mathcal{F}\).
659	\end{lemma}
660	
661	\begin{proof}
662	  
663	  Suppose \(\alpha(T)\) sends both \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle\) and
664	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\) to the same \(T\)-point of the
665	  Grassmannian. Then the pulled-back kernel
666	  $(\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \to (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* E_T(r)
667	  = \pi_{T_0}^* (W \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^r V)_T$
668	  is the same. The diagram \textbf{(**)} of
669	  \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} gives a right-exact sequence
670	  \[
671	    (\pi_T)^* (\pi_T)_* \mathcal{G}_i(r) \xrightarrow{h}
672	    E_T(r) \xrightarrow{q_i(r)} \mathcal{F}_i(r) \to 0,
673	  \]
674	  so \(q_i(r)\) is recovered as the cokernel of \(h\); twisting by \(-r\)
675	  recovers \(q_i\), giving \(q_1 = q_2\) and hence
676	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}_1, q_1\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}_2, q_2\rangle\).
677	\end{proof}
678	
679	\begin{lemma}[Valuative criterion of properness for \(\Quot\)]
680	  \label{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}
681	  
682	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 5, sub-section "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
683	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
684	  % L2510-L2542). Original reference: EGA IV(2) 2.8.1, cited there.
685	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
686	  % "The functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) satisfies the following valuative
687	  %  criterion for properness over \(S\): given any discrete valuation ring
688	  %  \(R\) over \(S\) with quotient field \(K\), the restriction map
689	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R) \to \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\(\)
690	  %  is bijective. This can be seen as follows.
691	  %  Given any coherent quotient
692	  %  \(q: E_K \to \F\) on \(X_R\) which defines an element \(\langle \F, q\rangle\)
693	  %  of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec K)\).
694	  %  Let \(\ov{\F}\) be the image of the composite homomorphism
695	  %  \(E_R \to j_*(E_K) \to j_*\F\) where \(j : X_K \hra X_R\) is the open
696	  %  inclusion. Let \(\ov{q} : E_R\to \ov{\F}\) be the induced surjection.
697	  %  Then we leave it to the reader to verify that
698	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) is an element of \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}(\spec R)\)
699	  %  which maps to \(\langle \F, q\rangle\), and is the unique such element.
700	  %  (Use the basic fact that being flat over a dvr
701	  %  is the same as being torsion-free.)"
702	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 5, "Valuative Criterion for Properness"
703	  (FGA Explained Ch.~5); EGA IV(2) 2.8.1.}
704	  The functor \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) satisfies the valuative criterion
705	  for properness over \(S\): for every discrete valuation ring \(R\) with
706	  fraction field \(K\) and every morphism \(\Spec R \to S\), the restriction
707	  map
708	  \[
709	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec R)
710	    \longrightarrow
711	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}(\Spec K)
712	  \]
713	  is bijective.
714	\end{lemma}
715	
716	\begin{proof}
717	  
718	  Given a \(\Spec K\)-point \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) on \(X_K\),
719	  let \(j : X_K \hookrightarrow X_R\) be the open inclusion. Define
720	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) as the image of the composite
721	  \(E_R \to j_* E_K \to j_* \mathcal{F}_K\), with induced surjection
722	  \(\overline{q} : E_R \to \overline{\mathcal{F}}\). The sheaf
723	  \(\overline{\mathcal{F}}\) is torsion-free over \(R\) (being a subsheaf of
724	  \(j_* \mathcal{F}_K\)), hence \(R\)-flat (flatness over a DVR
725	  \(=\) torsion-freeness), and restricts on the generic fiber to
726	  \(\mathcal{F}_K\). The pair \((\overline{\mathcal{F}}, \overline{q})\) is
727	  the unique extension of \(\langle \mathcal{F}_K, q_K\rangle\) to a
728	  \(\Spec R\)-point (uniqueness: any other extension agrees on the dense
729	  open \(X_K \subset X_R\) and is torsion-free, hence equals the image
730	  construction). The Hilbert polynomial is constant on connected
731	  \(\Spec R\) by flatness.
732	\end{proof}

734	\section{Cohomology and base change}
735	\label{sec:cohomology_base_change}
736	
737	The Quot construction uses cohomology-and-base-change in two places: the
738	boundedness step (\cref{lem:quot_boundedness}) and the Grassmannian
739	embedding (\cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective}). We record the relevant
740	statement as a named theorem so the Lean encoding can cite it directly.
741	
742	\begin{theorem}\leanok
743	[Flat base change of cohomology]
744	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChangeCohomology}
746	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology)
747	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947-L970).
748	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
749	  % "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
750	  %  \(\)
751	  %  \xymatrix{
752	  %  X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
753	  %  S' \ar[r]^g & S
754	  %  }
755	  %  \(\)
756	  %  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
757	  %  with pullback \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\).
758	  %  Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
759	  %  For any \(i \geq 0\)
760	  %  \begin{enumerate}
761	  %  \item the base change map of
762	  %  Cohomology, Lemma REF
763	  %  is an isomorphism
764	  %  \(\)
765	  %  g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
766	  %  \(\)
767	  %  \item if \(S = \Spec(A)\) and \(S' = \Spec(B)\), then
768	  %  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\).
769	  %  \end{enumerate}"
770	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 02KH (lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology).}
771	  Let
772	  \[
773	    \begin{array}{ccc}
774	      X' & \xrightarrow{g'} & X \\
775	      \downarrow f' &  & \downarrow f \\
776	      S' & \xrightarrow{g} & S
777	    \end{array}
778	  \]
779	  be a cartesian diagram of schemes with \(g\) flat and \(f\) quasi-compact and
780	  quasi-separated. Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
781	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module with pull-back
782	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^* \mathcal{F}\). For every \(i \ge 0\):
783	  \begin{enumerate}
784	    \item the base-change map
785	      \(g^* R^i f_* \mathcal{F} \to R^i f'_* \mathcal{F}'\) is an isomorphism;
786	    \item if \(S = \Spec A\) and \(S' = \Spec B\), then $H^i(X, \mathcal{F})
787	      \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
788	  \end{enumerate}
789	\end{theorem}
790	
791	\subsection{Project-side base-change substrate}
792	\label{subsec:quot_project_basechange}
793	
794	The Lean encoding of \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} (and the
795	Quot construction's iso between pushforward and tensor-pullback for the
796	universal section pattern) relies on four project-side declarations
797	introduced in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. These
798	are typed-sorry today: each carries a substantive Lean type that
799	encodes the intended mathematical statement, with the body deferred to
800	later iters (see \cref{sec:quot_lean_encoding} for the
801	phase-by-phase plan).
802

803	\begin{definition}\leanok
804	[Canonical base-change map on sheaves of modules]
805	  \label{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
806	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap}
807	  \uses{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
808	  Given a cartesian square of schemes
809	  \(\square = (g' : X' \to X,\ f' : X' \to S',\ f : X \to S,\ g : S' \to S)\)
810	  with \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) and \(g\) flat, the
811	  \emph{canonical base-change map} on sheaves of modules is the natural
812	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \longrightarrow f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\)
813	  arising from the universal property of pullback. Encoded as a morphism
814	  of \(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\)-valued sheaf functors on the pulled-back open
815	  cover; this is the morphism whose iso-status discharges
816	  \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}(1) in the present
817	  finite-locally-free / quasi-coherent-of-finite-type setting.
818	\end{definition}
819	
820	\begin{theorem}\leanok
821	[Stalkwise base-change isomorphism on affines]
822	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
823	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_app_app_isIso}
824	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map, thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}
825	  For every affine open \(V \subseteq S'\) and every affine open
826	  \(U \subseteq X\) lying over \(V\), the local component
827	  \((\text{canonicalBaseChangeMap}\ \square).\text{app}\ \mathcal{F}.\text{app}\ U\)
828	  of \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism. Proof
829	  reduces (Stacks 02KH(2)) to the affine-base case: \(H^0\) commutes with
830	  flat extension of scalars on a quasi-compact quasi-separated affine
831	  morphism.
832	\end{theorem}
833	
834	\begin{theorem}\leanok
835	[Canonical base-change map is a sheaf isomorphism]
836	  \label{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_isIso}
837	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.canonicalBaseChangeMap_isIso}
838	  \uses{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}
839	  Globalising the stalk-wise statement
840	  \cref{thm:quot_canonical_basechange_app_app_isIso}: the natural
841	  transformation \(g'^* f^* \mathcal{F} \to f'^* g^* \mathcal{F}\) of
842	  \cref{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map} is an isomorphism of
843	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules. This is the sheaf-level form of
844	  \cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology}(1).
845	\end{theorem}
846	
847	\begin{definition}\leanok
848	[Tensor-presentation of the pullback-of-sections functor]
849	  \label{def:quot_pullback_app_isoTensor}
850	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback_app_isoTensor}
851	  \uses{def:quot_canonical_basechange_map}
852	  Given a morphism \(g : X \to Y\) of schemes, affine opens
853	  \(U \subseteq Y\), \(V \subseteq X\) with \(g(V) \subseteq U\), and a
854	  quasi-coherent sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{N}\) on \(Y\), the
855	  \emph{tensor-presentation iso} packages the canonical iso
856	  \(\Gamma(V,\, g^* \mathcal{N}) \;\cong\;
857	   \Gamma(V,\, \mathcal{O}_X) \otimes_{\Gamma(U,\, \mathcal{O}_Y)}
858	     \Gamma(U,\, \mathcal{N})\)
859	  as a `LinearEquiv`. This is the project-side rfl-bridge used by the
860	  consumer \(\text{Quot}\)-functor iso assembly: it discharges the
861	  pullback-app step of the Tilde-isoTop route (Stacks tag 01HQ / 01I8)
862	  to a base-change-of-scalars identity. The body factors as
863	  (Step 1, axiom-clean): the underlying linear map
864	  \texttt{pullback\_app\_isoTensor\_unitAtV};
865	  (Step 2, project-side typed sorry): the
866	  \texttt{IsBaseChange}-packaging step
867	  \texttt{pullback\_app\_isoTensor\_isBaseChange} reducing to
868	  Stacks 02KE bijectivity of the canonical map.

871	\begin{definition}
872	
873	\leanok
874	[\(\Sigma\)-pair packaging: section-level LinearEquiv + intertwining]
875	  \label{def:pullback_app_isoTensor_sigma}
876	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_sectionLinearEquiv}
877	  \uses{def:quot_pullback_app_isoTensor, def:quot_canonical_basechange_map,
878	        lem:pullback_tildeIso, lem:tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen,
879	        lem:pullback_of_openImmersion_iso_restrict}
880	  Iter-188 Lane F-introduced \(\Sigma\)-pair packaging declaration that
881	  bundles BOTH the section-level LinearEquiv
882	  \(\Gamma(V,(g^*\mathcal N)) \;\simeq_{\Gamma(Y,U)}\; \Gamma(Y,U) \otimes
883	    \Gamma(N,V)\) AND its intertwining property with the canonical
884	  base-change map into a single \(\Sigma\)-struct returned via
885	  \texttt{Nonempty} for \texttt{IsBaseChange.of\_equiv} consumption.
886	  Concretely:
887	  \[
888	    \exists\, (f : \Gamma(V,(\text{pullback}\, g).\mathrm{obj}\,
889	                     \mathcal N) \;\simeq_{\Gamma(Y,U)}\;
890	                     \Gamma(X,V) \otimes_{\Gamma(Y,U)} \Gamma(N,V)),\;
891	      \forall x,\; f(1\otimes x)\;=\;\text{baseMap}\,g\,\mathcal N\,e\,x.
892	  \]
893	  This packaging idiom keeps consumer signatures clean and is the project-
894	  side bridge into Mathlib's \texttt{IsBaseChange.of\_equiv} consumer.
895	  Body is gated on \cref{lem:pullback_tildeIso} (Stacks 01HQ) plus
896	  Beck-Chevalley intertwining; iter-189+ build via the 5-step Tilde-isoTop
897	  route (\cref{lem:pullback_tildeIso} + \texttt{hU.isoSpec} +
898	  \texttt{tilde.isoTop} + Spec-restriction transport + adjunction-unit
899	  naturality).
900	\end{definition}

1078	\section{Lean encoding}
1079	\label{sec:quot_lean_encoding}
1080	
1081	The Lean target file is the new
1082	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean}. The construction
1083	proceeds in three phases.
1084	
1085	\paragraph{Phase 1: Hilbert polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}).}
1086	On a finite-type morphism \(\pi : X \to S\) with \(S\) noetherian and a
1087	coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) on \(X\) with proper support over \(S\), the per-fiber
1088	Hilbert polynomial is encoded as a function $s \mapsto
1089	\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]$. Mathlib provides
1090	\texttt{CategoryTheory.PolynomialModule} and Euler-characteristic
1091	infrastructure on coherent sheaves on noetherian schemes; the polynomial
1092	property of \(m \mapsto \chi(\mathcal{F} \otimes L^{\otimes m})\) requires
1093	Snapper's Lemma, which is a project-side sub-build on top of the
1094	graded-Euler-characteristic infrastructure.
1095	
1096	\paragraph{Phase 2: Grassmannian scheme (\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme},
1097	\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).} The Grassmannian
1098	\(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) is encoded as the relative gluing of \(\binom{r}{d}\)
1099	affine charts \(U^I\) (each isomorphic to a relative affine space
1100	\(\mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_S\)) via the Pl\"ucker cocycle \(\theta_{I,J}\). The
1101	gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData}. The
1102	universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\) is glued by the transition cocycle
1103	\(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), and the determinant line bundle
1104	\(\det \mathcal{U}\) gives the Pl\"ucker closed embedding into
1105	\(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\). The representability statement uses
1106	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} (as in
1107	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) on the universal pair
1108	\((\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d), \mathcal{U})\).
1109	
1110	\paragraph{Phase 3: Quot scheme (\cref{thm:quot_representable}).}
1111	The Quot functor is constructed as a locally closed subfunctor of the
1112	Grassmannian
1113	\(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\), via:
1114	\begin{enumerate}
1115	  \item the uniform \(m\)-regularity bound from
1116	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness} (input: Castelnuovo--Mumford theorem,
1117	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.CastelnuovoMumford} -- project-side sub-build);
1118	  \item the Grassmannian embedding \(\alpha\) from
1119	    \cref{lem:quot_alpha_injective};
1120	  \item the flattening stratification (\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
1121	    of \cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}, A.2.a) applied to the
1122	    universal quotient on the Grassmannian, producing the locally closed
1123	    stratum \(T_0^\Phi\);
1124	  \item the valuative-criterion upgrade
1125	    (\cref{lem:quot_valuative_criterion}) to closed embedding, hence
1126	    projective \(S\)-scheme.
1127	\end{enumerate}
1128	The reduction \cref{lem:quot_reduction_to_pi_star_W} from the universal
1129	case to the general case is encoded as a closed embedding of representable
1130	subfunctors via the kernel-vanishing scheme.
1131	
1132	\paragraph{Cohomology and base change.}
1133	\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology} is the Mathlib-equivalent of
1134	Stacks tag 02KH. Mathlib carries a partial form
1135	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.flat\_base\_change}) for affine bases; the
1136	full \(R^i f_*\) statement is a project-side bridge if not present at the
1137	pinned commit.
1138	
1139	\paragraph{Dependencies summary.}
1140	This file directly consumes
1141	\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec} (A.1.a, on disk) for relative-spectrum
1142	gluing infrastructure and
1143	\cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} from
1144	\cref{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification} (A.2.a, written this iter)
1145	for the flattening stratum. Downstream, \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
1146	(A.2.c) consumes \cref{thm:quot_representable} via the special case
1147	\(E = \mathcal{O}_C\) on a smooth proper curve \(C/k\), giving the Hilbert
1148	scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot^{\Phi,\mathcal{O}_C(1)}_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
1149	and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective divisors.
1150	
1151	\section{Out of scope}
1152	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
1153	
1154	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-scheme existence statement and
1155	its proof skeleton via the Grassmannian + flattening-stratification
1156	route. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
1157	\begin{itemize}
1158	  \item \textbf{Flattening stratification} (A.2.a) -- consumed-by, not
1159	    proved-here. Its blueprint home is the new chapter
1160	    \texttt{Picard\_FlatteningStratification.tex}.
1161	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
1162	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
1163	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot scheme and is
1164	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
1165	  \item \textbf{Castelnuovo--Mumford \(m\)-regularity theorem} -- input to
1166	    \cref{lem:quot_boundedness}, not proved here. The proof lives in
1167	    Nitsure \S 2; for the Lean encoding it is a project-side sub-build.
1168	  \item \textbf{Semi-continuity and base change} (Nitsure \S 3) -- the
1169	    flat-base-change theorem of cohomology (\cref{thm:flat_base_change_cohomology})
1170	    is stated here as a lemma, but the upstream Mathlib bridge / project-side
1171	    sub-build is not constructed in this chapter.
1172	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
1173	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
1174	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
1175	    project-side sub-build for the Lean encoding.
1176	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
1177	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
1178	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.

1	# Analogy: `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` path + 5-step Tilde-isoTop Mathlib alignment for Lane F
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	lane-f-isbasechange
8	
9	## Iteration
10	189
11	
12	## Question
13	
14	(from iter-189 directive) Three sub-questions:
15	
16	1. Is `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` the correct Mathlib API path for proving
17	   `IsBaseChange e (pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap g N e)` at Lane F, or
18	   should the project switch to `IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique` or a
19	   different constructor?
20	2. What is the cleanest Mathlib-aligned recipe for the `Nonempty Σ-pair`
21	   signature of `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_sectionLinearEquiv`?
22	3. What are the Mathlib lemmas behind each of the 5 steps of the Tilde-
23	   isoTop route (Steps 1 = `N|_V ≅ tilde Γ(N,V)`; 2 = pullback-tildeIso;
24	   3 = `hU.isoSpec` transport; 4 = `tilde.isoTop`; 5 = adjunction-unit
25	   naturality)?
26	
27	Verdict needed: (A) STRUCTURAL OK / (B) STRUCTURAL ALIGN / (C) STRUCTURAL
28	BLOCKED.
29	
30	## Project artifact(s)
31	
32	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean:597-649` —
33	  `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_sectionLinearEquiv` (iter-188 NAMED
34	  helper, body = typed `sorry`).
35	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean:651-695` —
36	  `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_isBaseChange` (iter-188 axiom-clean
37	  body using `IsBaseChange.of_equiv`).
38	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean:543-571` —
39	  `pullback_tildeIso` (Step-2 typed-sorry pin).
40	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean:573-595` —
41	  `pushforward_isQuasicoherent` (Stacks 01XJ, typed-sorry pin).
42	- Pre-existing analogy: `analogies/quotscheme-isbasechange-tilde.md`
43	  (iter-187 verdict).
44	
45	## Mathlib precedents examined (b80f227)
46	
47	| Mathlib symbol | Location | What it gives |
48	|---|---|---|
49	| `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` | `Mathlib/RingTheory/IsTensorProduct.lean:394` | `(e : S ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[S] N) → (∀ x, e (1 ⊗ₜ x) = f x) → IsBaseChange S f` |
50	| `IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique` | same file:428 | takes universal property `∀ Q [...], ∀ g : M →ₗ[R] Q, ∃! g' : N →ₗ[S] Q, ...` |
51	| `IsBaseChange.ofEquiv` (capital E) | same file:473 | `(e : M ≃ₗ[R] N) → IsBaseChange R e.toLinearMap` — only for the trivial `R → R` base change |
52	| `IsBaseChange.iff_lift_unique` | same file:461 | the iff version of `of_lift_unique` |
53	| `TensorProduct.isBaseChange` | same file:363 | `IsBaseChange S (TensorProduct.mk R S M 1)` — the canonical witness |
54	| `IsBaseChange.equiv` | same file:375 | `IsBaseChange S f → S ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[S] N` — the consumer used by `pullback_app_isoTensor_isBaseChange.equiv.symm` |
55	| `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.isoTop` | `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Tilde.lean:177` | `M ≅ (modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤)` |
56	| `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.adjunction` | same file:279 | `tilde.functor R ⊣ moduleSpecΓFunctor`; unit is `IsIso` (instance at 307) |
57	| `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.fullyFaithfulFunctor` | same file:312 | `tilde` is fully faithful |
58	| `AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff` | same file:340 | `IsIso M.fromTildeΓ ↔ essImage tilde M` |
59	| `AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation` | same file:398 | `(M : (Spec R).Modules).Presentation → IsIso M.fromTildeΓ` |
60	| `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` | `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/Quasicoherent.lean:249` | typeclass: `Nonempty (QuasicoherentData M)` (∃ cover + presentations on EACH cover element) |
61	| `SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData.presentation` | same file:208 | per-cover-element presentation; NO direct extraction on a specific affine open V |
62	| `AlgebraicGeometry.IsAffineOpen.isoSpec` | `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/AffineScheme.lean:380` | `U ≅ Spec Γ(X, U)` for `U` affine |

64	
65	## Decisions identified
66	
67	### Decision 1: `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` vs `of_lift_unique` vs `ofEquiv`
68	
69	- **Mathlib idiom**: For "I have an explicit equiv `S ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[S] N`
70	  and a 1-tensor intertwining property", the canonical constructor is
71	  `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` (`IsTensorProduct.lean:394`). The 1-tensor
72	  intertwining `∀ x, e (1 ⊗ₜ x) = f x` is exactly the data needed.
73	
74	  `IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique` is for the *universal-property* path:
75	  you must produce, for every target `Q`, a unique extension of every
76	  `R`-linear `M → Q` to an `S`-linear `N → Q`. That is structurally
77	  harder to discharge when the equiv is constructed concretely on the
78	  Spec side.
79	
80	  `IsBaseChange.ofEquiv` (capital E) only handles `e : M ≃ₗ[R] N`
81	  (same scalar ring) — gives `IsBaseChange R e.toLinearMap`. Inapplicable
82	  to Lane F's `R = Γ(X,V) → S = Γ(Y,U)` setup.
83	
84	- **Project's current path** (`QuotScheme.lean:672-695`): produces a
85	  `Nonempty {f : Γ(X,V) ⊗ Γ(N,V) ≃ₗ[Γ(Y,U)] Γ((pullback g).obj N, U) //
86	   ∀ x, f (1 ⊗ₜ x) = pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap g N e x}` via the
87	  named helper, then dispatches `IsBaseChange.of_equiv equiv hApp`.
88	
89	- **Gap**: identical. The project's path is exactly the Mathlib idiom.
90	
91	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** — `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` is the correct API
92	  path. The other constructors are structurally heavier or inapplicable.
93	
94	### Decision 2: `Nonempty Σ-pair` packaging idiom
95	
96	- **Mathlib idiom**: Mathlib has no `LinearEquiv.intertwines` predicate.
97	  The closest idioms are:
98	  (a) Return data + Prop as TWO declarations (a `noncomputable def`
99	      producing the equiv, a `theorem` recording the intertwining).
100	  (b) Return `∃ f : LinearEquiv ..., ∀ x, ...` (a Prop with a Type-valued
101	      witness — perfectly legal since `LinearEquiv` is a `Type`, not
102	      `Prop`).
103	  (c) Return `Nonempty {f : LinearEquiv // ∀ x, ...}` — the project's
104	      current choice. Definitionally a heavier wrapping of (b).
105	
106	- **Project's current path**: option (c). Consumer in
107	  `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_isBaseChange` (L691) unpacks via
108	  `obtain ⟨equiv, hApp⟩ := ...` then feeds into `IsBaseChange.of_equiv`.
109	
110	- **Gap**: divergent-equivalent. (c) and (b) are interchangeable; (c)
111	  is mildly noisier (an extra `Subtype.mk` layer when constructing).
112	  Mathlib leans toward (b) for pure-existence statements (`∃ e, ...`).
113	  Once the helper body is closed, the natural endpoint is *not* (b)
114	  or (c) but (a): a `noncomputable def` for the equiv + a `simp` lemma
115	  for `(1 ⊗ₜ x) = baseMap x`. But while the body is a typed sorry,
116	  packaging as `Nonempty` (which only asserts inhabitedness, not
117	  computability) is the right hygiene.
118	
119	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** with current `Nonempty {f // ...}` packaging.
120	  Optionally consider switching to bare `∃ f, ...` for syntactic
121	  parsimony (saves ~1 LOC in consumer obtain pattern). NOT a blocker.
122	
123	### Decision 3: 5-step Tilde-isoTop Mathlib lemmas per step
124	
125	| Step | Project description | Mathlib lemma(s) at b80f227 | Status |
126	|---|---|---|---|
127	| 1 | `N\|_V ≅ tilde Γ(N, V)` on `Spec Γ(X, V)` | Composition: `isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff` (`Tilde.lean:340`) ↔ `essImage tilde`; OR `isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation` (`Tilde.lean:398`) from a `Presentation`. The "extract a presentation of `(N\|_V).overSpec` from `[N.IsQuasicoherent]`" sub-step is a **Mathlib gap** (Stacks 01I8: QC ⟺ tilde on every affine open). `IsQuasicoherent` only supplies a cover + presentations on COVER elements (`Quasicoherent.lean:208`), not on a chosen affine open `V`. | NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL |
128	| 2 | Pullback via `Spec.map φ`: `(pullback (Spec.map φ)).obj (tilde M) ≅ tilde (B ⊗_A M)` | None at b80f227. Direct LSP searches (`pullback.*tilde`, `tilde.*pullback`, `baseChange.*tilde`) return zero matches. Project pin: `pullback_tildeIso` (Stacks 01HQ / 0BJ8). | NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (project pin in place) |
129	| 3 | Transport via `hU.isoSpec` back to `U`-sections | Combines `IsAffineOpen.isoSpec` (`AffineScheme.lean:380`), `IsAffineOpen.fromSpec` (`AffineScheme.lean:414`), and "module-pullback along an open immersion is restriction" — the LAST piece is a **Mathlib gap**: there is no `Scheme.Modules.pullback_of_openImmersion_iso_restriction` lemma at b80f227. The conceptual content is that `Scheme.Modules.pullback U.ι ≅ Scheme.Modules.restrictionToOpen U`. | NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (no project pin yet) |
130	| 4 | Evaluate at `⊤` via `tilde.isoTop` | `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.isoTop` (`Tilde.lean:177`). **MATHLIB HAS.** | OK |
131	| 5 | Naturality of adjunction unit (1-tensor intertwining) | `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.adjunction` (`Tilde.lean:279`) + `IsIso (tilde.adjunction.unit)` instance (`Tilde.lean:307`). The specific compatibility with `pullback_app_isoTensor_unitAtV` is project-side glue (~20-40 LOC). | OK (Mathlib supplies the adjunction; specific intertwining is project glue) |
132	
133	- **Verdict per step**:
134	  - Step 4: **PROCEED** — Mathlib lemma in place.
135	  - Step 5: **PROCEED** — Mathlib adjunction in place; intertwining is
136	    project glue (no Mathlib gap; just naturality bookkeeping).
137	  - Steps 1, 2, 3: **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL**. Of these, Step 2 has a
138	    project pin (`pullback_tildeIso`). Steps 1 and 3 currently do NOT
139	    have separate pins — their substantive content is bundled into the
140	    body of `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_sectionLinearEquiv`.
141	
142	### Decision 4: Pin Steps 1 and 3 as separate typed sorries (refactor)
143	
144	- **Mathlib idiom**: Mathlib's pattern for substantive missing
145	  infrastructure is one named declaration per Stacks tag, so future
146	  porting-to-Mathlib lands one PR per tag.
147	
148	- **Project's current path**: All three Mathlib gaps (Steps 1, 2, 3)
149	  are conceptually independent, but only Step 2 is split out. Steps 1
150	  and 3 are buried inside `_sectionLinearEquiv`'s body sorry, making
151	  the `K=4 iters / 9→11 sorry creep / 6 helpers / PARTIAL×4` STUCK
152	  pattern (per iter-188 progress-critic) hard to break: the prover
153	  cannot make progress on `_sectionLinearEquiv` because closing it
154	  requires *three* substantive Stacks lemmas, only ONE of which has a
155	  name to put in scope.
156	
157	- **Gap**: divergent-with-cost. Cost: provers iterate on the bundled
158	  helper without a way to localize which Stacks gap is the residual
159	  obstruction; the typed-sorry budget keeps growing without progress.
160	
161	- **Verdict**: **ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB** — split out Step 1 and Step 3 as
162	  separately-named typed-sorry pins, parallel to `pullback_tildeIso`
163	  for Step 2. After this refactor, the body of `_sectionLinearEquiv`
164	  becomes pure compositional glue with no internal Mathlib gaps, and
165	  prover work can focus on the three named pins independently.
166	
167	## Recommendation
168	
169	**iter-189 blueprint + prover dispatch for Lane F**:
170	
171	1. **Confirm**: the `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` API path taken in iter-188
172	   is correct. No pivot needed. The structural choice is the right
173	   Mathlib idiom. (Decisions 1, 2.)
174	
175	2. **Refactor (high priority)**: split out two new typed-sorry pins
176	   in `Picard/QuotScheme.lean`, parallel to the existing
177	   `pullback_tildeIso`:
178	
179	   - `tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen` (Step 1):
180	     given `[N.IsQuasicoherent]` and `hV : IsAffineOpen V`, produce
181	     `Nonempty (N|_V ≅ tilde Γ(N, V) /* on Spec Γ(X, V) */)`.
182	     Stacks 01I8 content. ~20-40 LOC body.
183	
184	   - `pullback_of_openImmersion_iso_restrict` (Step 3):
185	     given an open immersion `j : U ⟶ X` (specifically `U.ι`), produce
186	     `Nonempty (Scheme.Modules.pullback j ≅ Scheme.Modules.restrictionToOpen U)`,
187	     or equivalently a section-level naturality lemma packaging the
188	     `hU.isoSpec` transport. Stacks gap. ~30-50 LOC body.
189	
190	3. **After the refactor**, the body of `_sectionLinearEquiv` becomes
191	   pure glue: chain Step 1 + Step 2 (`pullback_tildeIso`) + Step 3
192	   transport + Step 4 (`tilde.isoTop`) + Step 5 (naturality), using
193	   `LinearEquiv.trans`. Estimated ~50-80 LOC of glue. The single typed
194	   sorry on `_sectionLinearEquiv` then evaporates — its body closes
195	   axiom-clean given the three named-pin Mathlib gaps.
196	
197	4. **Blueprint expansion** (`Picard_QuotScheme.tex`): add Lean-4-
198	   friendly decomposition matching the three named pins. The blueprint
199	   currently has `def:pullback_app_isoTensor_sigma` as a plan-phase pin;
200	   expand it to explicitly enumerate the three Mathlib-gap steps and
201	   the two Mathlib-supplied steps (4 & 5).
202	
203	5. **Do NOT** switch to `IsBaseChange.of_lift_unique`. The universal-
204	   property hypothesis would require the same Mathlib gaps (no shortcut)
205	   AND introduce an extra layer of `∃!` bookkeeping. The project's
206	   `of_equiv` path is strictly cleaner.
207	
208	## Verdict summary
209	
210	| Decision | Verdict | Severity |
211	|---|---|---|
212	| 1. `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` API path | PROCEED (identical to Mathlib idiom) | informational |
213	| 2. `Nonempty Σ-pair` packaging | PROCEED (divergent-equivalent; bare `∃ f, ...` slightly cleaner but not a blocker) | informational |
214	| 3. 5-step Tilde route — Steps 4, 5 | PROCEED (Mathlib supplies the lemmas) | informational |
215	| 3. 5-step Tilde route — Steps 1, 2, 3 | NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (Step 2 has a project pin; Steps 1 & 3 do NOT) | high |
216	| 4. Pin Steps 1 and 3 separately | ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB (refactor) | critical (unbundles the STUCK helper) |
217	
218	**Overall verdict**: **(A) STRUCTURAL OK** with a critical refactor
219	recommendation. The `IsBaseChange.of_equiv` choice is structurally
220	correct and does NOT need a pivot. The reason iter-188's helper has
221	been STUCK across 4 iters is *not* because the API path is wrong, but
222	because three independent Mathlib gaps are bundled into one named
223	helper, leaving provers without separate targets. Pinning Steps 1 and
224	3 separately (parallel to Step 2's existing `pullback_tildeIso` pin)
225	breaks the STUCK pattern and makes the `_sectionLinearEquiv` body
226	axiom-clean glue.

import Mathlib

-- The R-module instance on Γ(tilde M, ⊤) exists via the modulesSpecToSheaf chain.
-- But also a natural one from being a section.
noncomputable example (R : CommRingCat) (M : ModuleCat R) :

-- Test: is `Γ(tilde M, ⊤)` already an R-module by inference?

    Module R Γ(tilde M, ⊤) := inferInstance

import Mathlib
open CategoryTheory AlgebraicGeometry

-- The smul compatibility I need
noncomputable example (R : CommRingCat) (M N : (Spec R).Modules) (φ : M ⟶ N) :
    letI : Algebra R Γ(Spec R, ⊤) := (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom.toAlgebra
    letI : Module R Γ(M, ⊤) :=
      Module.compHom _ (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom
    letI : Module R Γ(N, ⊤) :=
      Module.compHom _ (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom
    ∀ (r : R) (x : Γ(M, ⊤)),
      (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app φ ⊤).hom (r • x) = r • (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app φ ⊤).hom x := by
  letI algInst : Algebra R Γ(Spec R, ⊤) := (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom.toAlgebra
  letI modM : Module R Γ(M, ⊤) :=
    Module.compHom _ (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom
  letI modN : Module R Γ(N, ⊤) :=
    Module.compHom _ (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom
  intro r x
  -- LHS: φ_⊤ ((ΓSpecIso.inv r) • x) -- Module.compHom
  -- RHS: (ΓSpecIso.inv r) • φ_⊤ x -- Module.compHom
  change (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app φ ⊤).hom ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom r • x) =
         (Scheme.ΓSpecIso R).inv.hom r • (Scheme.Modules.Hom.app φ ⊤).hom x
  exact Scheme.Modules.Hom.app_smul φ _ x

     reducing to `Scheme.Modules.Hom.app_smul composedIso.hom`.
  4. `toTensor := topLin.trans (tilde.isoTop TR).symm.toLinearEquiv`
     (the underlying types `Γ(tilde TR, ⊤)` and `(modulesSpecToSheaf.obj
     (tilde TR)).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤)` are defeq; the Γ(Y,U)-module
     structures match via `Module.compHom` vs. `restrictScalars
     globalSectionsIso.hom`).
  5. `f := toTensor.symm.trans step3`.
- **Result**: RESOLVED for steps 1-5 (LinearEquiv construction axiom-clean).
- **Key insight**: `(tilde.isoTop M).toLinearEquiv` directly produces the
  needed `M ≃ₗ[R] Γ(tilde M, ⊤)` (the two Γ(Y,U)-module instances are
  defeq enough to make this typecheck without a manual `Module.compHom`
  bridge on the tilde side).

### Attempt 2 (line 957: Beck-Chevalley intertwining at `1 ⊗ₜ x`)
- **Approach**: Tried `rfl`, `simp [f, toTensor, topLin, topAdd,
  composedIso, pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap, ...]`, `unfold ...`.
- **Result**: FAILED (`rfl` unfolds the goal to a chain involving
  `Scheme.Modules.Hom.app step1.inv`, `Scheme.Modules.Hom.app step2.inv`,
  and `(tilde.isoTop TR).symm.toLinearEquiv.symm` applied to `1 ⊗ₜ x`,
  which cannot be closed by `rfl`/`simp` alone).
- **Architectural finding**: The intertwining provably DEPENDS on the
  bodies of `step1` (`tildeIso_of_isQuasicoherent_isAffineOpen`,
  Stacks 01I8) and `step2` (`pullback_tildeIso`, Stacks 01HQ). Both are
  currently `Nonempty (... ≅ ...)` typed-sorry pins; their isos are
  opaque (Classical.choice-extracted), so the LHS chain cannot be
  reduced to a closed form. **The intertwining is unprovable until
  either**:
  - (a) `step1`'s and `step2`'s bodies land (Mathlib gaps Stacks 01I8 +
    01HQ), OR
  - (b) `step1` and `step2` signatures are refactored to Σ-pair form
    carrying the canonical iso-characterizing identity (e.g.,
    `step1.hom = (asIso fromTildeΓ).inv` for QC-on-affine, or
    `step2.hom = canonical tilde-pullback-iso` for Spec base change).

### Mathlib precedent searches done
- `IsQuasicoherent.pushforward`: no direct API at pinned commit (Stacks
  01XJ unowned at `Scheme.Modules` layer per analogist verdict in
  `analogies/lane-f-isbasechange.md`).
- `Module.compHom` + `restrictScalars`: defeq verified via
  `lean_run_code` test (the Γ(Y,U)-module on `Γ(tilde M, ⊤)` via
  `Module.compHom _ ΓSpecIso.inv.hom` matches the natural one via
  `(modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤)`).
- `tilde.isoTop`: confirmed at `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Tilde.lean:177`,
  produces the canonical `M ≅ (modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (tilde M)).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤)`.
- `Scheme.Modules.Hom.app_smul`: confirmed at
  `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Sheaf.lean:110`, gives
  Γ(X,U)-linearity of `Hom.app φ U`.

### Status post-iter-194

- **Sorry count**: 12 (unchanged from iter-193).
- **Structural**: `_sectionLinearEquiv` body is now ~30 LOC of pure
  compositional glue (axiom-clean LinearEquiv construction) followed by
  ONE typed sorry on the intertwining. Pre-iter-194, the entire
  `Nonempty {f // ...}` was a single typed sorry. Post-iter-194, the
  LinearEquiv `f` is concretely built and the residual sorry is a
  focused identity `f (1 ⊗ₜ x) = baseMap g N e x`.

### Suggested iter-195+ next steps for the Plan agent

1. **Refactor step1/step2 to Σ-pair signatures** with canonical-iso
   identities. This is the unblocking refactor for `_sectionLinearEquiv`
   body closure. Cost: ~10-15 LOC per pin (the signature gains a
   `// step1.hom = canonical_map`-style clause; the bodies still typed
   sorry but the consumer can use the identity).
2. **OR**: Close `step1`'s body via Stacks 01I8 path (extract a
   Presentation of `(N|_V).overSpec` from `[N.IsQuasicoherent] + hV`
   transport, then apply `isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation`). Estimated
   ~50-80 LOC; Mathlib gap is the per-affine-open presentation
   extraction (`QuasicoherentData.presentation` only ships per-cover
   element).
3. Either path unblocks the Beck-Chevalley intertwining and closes
   `_sectionLinearEquiv` axiom-clean, which then propagates to
   `pullback_app_isoTensor_isBaseChange` and `Scheme.Modules.pullback_app_isoTensor`.

### HARD BAR status

iter-194 HARD BAR was "≥1 axiom-clean closure". This was NOT met
(no full closure of any typed sorry); however, **substantial structural
progress** landed:
- The LinearEquiv extraction (Steps a-c of the iter-189 5-step plan)
  is now axiom-clean.
- The body of `_sectionLinearEquiv` no longer hides three Mathlib gaps
  + bookkeeping under one bare sorry; the sorry is now narrowed to the
  Beck-Chevalley intertwining at `1 ⊗ₜ x` (a single specific identity).
- An architectural finding was surfaced: the intertwining is provably
  blocked on step1/step2 body landings OR signature strengthening —
  this is concrete guidance for iter-195+ plan-phase routing.

PUSH-BEYOND (full `_sectionLinearEquiv` close) was NOT achievable this
iter without prior step1/step2 work.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).

10	/-!
11	# Vanishing of `H¹` for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H¹)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.2.H¹** project-side build of the closed-point
14	skyscraper `H¹`-vanishing identity
15	
16	  `dim_{k̄} H¹(C, skyscraperSheaf P (ModuleCat.of k̄ k̄)) = 0`
17	
18	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`. The file is
19	the iter-191 Lane H **file-skeleton**: each of the eight pinned
20	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching
21	the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin in `chapters/RiemannRoch_H1Vanishing.tex`),
22	with `sorry` bodies; the iter-192+ closure follows the classical
23	Hartshorne III.2.5 flasque-vanishing argument.
24	
25	The chapter strategy (Hartshorne III §2): an injective resolution of a
26	flasque sheaf yields a flasque quotient at each stage; the
27	global-sections functor is exact on a flasque-to-flasque-to-flasque
28	short exact sequence; hence the right-derived global-sections functor
29	vanishes on a flasque input in positive degree. We specialise the
30	abstract flasque-vanishing to the closed-point skyscraper sheaf, which
31	is flasque because it is the pushforward, along the closed embedding of
32	a one-point subspace, of the constant sheaf on an irreducible base.
33	
34	## Eight pinned declarations
35	
36	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque` — predicate on
37	   `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`.
38	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward` — pushforward of a
39	   flasque sheaf is flasque (Hartshorne II.1, Ex. 1.16(d)).
40	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible` —
41	   constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque (Hartshorne
42	   II.1, Ex. 1.16(a)).
43	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero` — flasque sheaves
44	   have zero `HModule` in positive degree (Hartshorne III.2.5).
45	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const` —
46	   skyscraper sheaf is the pushforward of a constant sheaf along the
47	   closed embedding of the closure of the support point.
48	6. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible` — the
49	   closure of the support point of a `PrimeDivisor` is irreducible.
50	7. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque` —
51	   closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque.
52	8. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero` — the
53	   `RR.2.H¹` headline (`dim_{k̄} H¹(C, k(P)) = 0`), obtained by composing
54	   declarations 4 and 7.
55	
56	The eighth declaration also lives as a `private` typed-`sorry` helper at
57	`AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean`. The `private` modifier
58	mangles the internal name of the RRFormula copy, so the public
59	declaration in this file is the one resolved by the blueprint's
60	`\lean{...}` pin and by downstream consumers (`sync_leanok` keys on the
61	fully-qualified public name).
62	
63	## References
64	
65	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_H1Vanishing.tex`.
66	Source: Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, II.1, Exercise 1.16
67	(flasque sheaves), Exercise 1.17 (skyscraper sheaves);
68	III.2, Proposition 2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic).
69	-/

80	/-! ## §1. Flasque sheaves of `kbar`-modules -/
81	
82	/-- **Flasque sheaf of `kbar`-modules on a topological space**
83	(Hartshorne II.1, Exercise 1.16; III.2, paragraph preceding Lemma 2.4).
84	
85	A sheaf `F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`
86	is **flasque** when, for every inclusion of opens `V ≤ U` in `X`, the
87	restriction map
88	`F.val.obj (op U) → F.val.obj (op V)` is surjective as a map of
89	`kbar`-modules.
90	
91	The predicate is read off the underlying presheaf of `kbar`-modules and
92	applies uniformly to the project's `ModuleCat kbar`-flavoured cohomology
93	pipeline (it does not depend on any topological-space hypothesis on `X`,
94	so the curve specialisation is by instance synthesis on
95	`C.left.toTopCat`).
96	
97	Blueprint reference: `def:isFlasque_sheaf`. -/
98	def Scheme.IsFlasque
99	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X : TopCat.{u}}
100	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : Prop :=
101	  ∀ ⦃U V : TopologicalSpace.Opens X⦄ (h : V ≤ U),
102	    Function.Surjective ((F.val.map (homOfLE h).op).hom)
103	
104	/-- **Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque** (Hartshorne II.1,
105	Exercise 1.16(d)).
106	
107	For a continuous map of topological spaces `f : X ⟶ Y` and a flasque
108	sheaf `F` of `kbar`-modules on `X`, the pushforward sheaf `f _* F` is
109	flasque on `Y`. The reason is purely formal: the restriction map of
110	`f _* F` along `V ≤ U` in `Y` is, by definition of pushforward, the
111	restriction map of `F` along `f ⁻¹ V ≤ f ⁻¹ U` in `X`, which is
112	surjective by hypothesis on `F`.
113	
114	**iter-191 Lane H prover dispatch** — closed via the unfolding
115	`pushforward.map (homOfLE h).op = F.val.map ((Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)).op`
116	(by `rfl`), so flasqueness of `F` along `(Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)` gives
117	the conclusion. Blueprint reference: `lem:isFlasque_pushforward`. -/
118	theorem Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward
119	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] {X Y : TopCat.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y)
120	    {F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology X) (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)}
121	    (hF : Scheme.IsFlasque F) :
122	    Scheme.IsFlasque
123	      ((TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) f).obj F) := by
124	  intro U V h
125	  exact hF ((Opens.map f).map (homOfLE h)).le
126	
127	/-- **Constant sheaf on an irreducible topological space is flasque**
