323	\section{Global sections as a Riemann--Roch space}
324	
325	\begin{lemma}
326	
327	
328	\leanok
329	[Global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) as rational functions with a
330	controlled pole]
331	  \label{lem:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.globalSections_iff}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.7, Proposition~7.7, p.~157 (effective
335	  % divisors linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) are the divisors of zeros of
336	  % global sections of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\), with the explicit
337	  % identification of those global sections with rational functions
338	  % \(f\) satisfying \((f) \geq -D_0\)) (read from
339	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 174).
340	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 7.7. \emph{Let \(X\) be a nonsingular
341	  % projective variety over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Let
342	  % \(D_0\) be a divisor on \(X\) and let \(\mathscr L = \mathscr L(D_0)\)
343	  % be the corresponding invertible sheaf. Then:
344	  % (a) for each nonzero \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\), the divisor of
345	  % zeros \((s)_0\) is an effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\);
346	  % (b) every effective divisor linearly equivalent to \(D_0\) is \((s)_0\)
347	  % for some \(s \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\); and
348	  % (c) two sections \(s, s' \in \Gamma(X, \mathscr L)\) have the same
349	  % divisor of zeros if and only if there is a \(\lambda \in k^*\) such
350	  % that \(s' = \lambda s\).}"
351	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(b) If \(D \geq 0\) and \(D = D_0 + (f)\), then
352	  % \((f) \geq -D_0\). Thus \(f\) gives a global section of
353	  % \(\mathscr L(D_0)\) whose divisor of zeros is \(D\)."
354	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.7, Proposition~7.7 and its
355	  proof of part (b), p.~157.}
356	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\), let \(P \in C\) be a
357	  closed point, and let \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) be the line bundle of
358	  REF. The \(\bar k\)-vector space of
359	  global sections of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is naturally identified with the
360	  Riemann--Roch space
361	  \[
362	    L([P]) \;:=\;
363	    \bigl\{f \in K(C)^{\times} \;\big|\; \div(f) + [P] \geq 0
364	    \text{ in } \mathrm{Div}(C)\bigr\}
365	    \,\cup\, \{0\}.
366	  \]
367	  Equivalently, the order conditions
368	  \[
369	    \ord_Q(f) \;\geq\; 0 \quad \text{for every closed point } Q \in C
370	    \text{ with } Q \neq P,
371	    \qquad
372	    \ord_P(f) \;\geq\; -1
373	  \]
374	  on \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) are equivalent to the existence of a global
375	  section \(s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) whose image \(\iota(s)\) under
376	  the canonical inclusion $\iota : \mathcal O_C(P) \hookrightarrow
377	  \mathcal K_C \cong K(C)\( equals \)f\( (the iff binds \)s\( to \)f$
378	  explicitly — vacuous-in-\(f\) readings such as ``some nonzero global
379	  section exists'' are excluded by construction).
380	
381	  Under this identification, the inclusion
382	  \(\bar k \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of constant functions
383	  corresponds to the natural map $\mathcal O_C \hookrightarrow
384	  \mathcal O_C(P)\( exhibiting \)\mathcal O_C(P)$ as the twist of the
385	  structure sheaf along the prime divisor \([P]\).
386	\end{lemma}
387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	  
390	  
391	  
392	\leanok
393	  By construction, \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module of
394	  the sheaf of total quotient rings \(\mathcal K_C\) generated locally by
395	  \(f_P^{-1}\) near \(P\) and by \(1\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), where \(f_P\) is
396	  a uniformizer at \(P\) (REF). Since \(C\)
397	  is integral, \(\mathcal K_C\) is the constant sheaf at the function field
398	  \(K(C)\), and a global section of any sub-\(\mathcal O_C\)-module
399	  \(\mathcal F \subset \mathcal K_C\) is precisely an element of \(K(C)\)
400	  whose germ at every point lies in \(\mathcal F\) at that point.
401	  Therefore \(f \in K(C)\) is a global section of \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) if
402	  and only if:
403	
404	  \begin{itemize}
405	    \item For every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\), the germ of
406	      \(f\) at \(Q\) lies in \(\mathcal O_C(P)_Q = \mathcal O_{C, Q}\)
407	      (the structure sheaf, since \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) agrees with
408	      \(\mathcal O_C\) on \(C \setminus \{P\}\)); equivalently,
409	      \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\).
410	    \item At the closed point \(P\), the germ of \(f\) at \(P\) lies in
411	      \(\mathcal O_C(P)_P = f_P^{-1} \cdot \mathcal O_{C, P}\);
412	      equivalently, \(f_P \cdot f \in \mathcal O_{C, P}\), i.e.\
413	      \(\ord_P(f_P \cdot f) \geq 0\), i.e.\ \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\).
414	  \end{itemize}
415	  In divisorial language (REF,
416	  REF), the two conditions combine to
417	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), recovering the Riemann--Roch space description
418	  \(L([P])\). The zero element of \(K(C)\) is included by convention as the
419	  zero global section.
420	
421	  The identification with Hartshorne's Proposition~7.7(b) is direct:
422	  taking \(D_0 = [P]\) effective, Hartshorne's argument shows that a
423	  rational function \(f\) with \((f) \geq -D_0\) defines a global section
424	  of \(\mathscr L(D_0)\). The condition \((f) \geq -D_0 = -[P]\) is exactly
425	  \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\). The compatibility with the constant inclusion
426	  follows by taking \(f = 1 \in K(C)^{\times}\): then \(\div(1) = 0\), so
427	  \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), and the corresponding section of
428	  \(\mathcal O_C(P)\) is the image of $1 \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
429	  \cong \bar k\( under the structural inclusion \)\mathcal O_C
430	  \hookrightarrow \mathcal O_C(P)$.
431	\end{proof}
432	
433	\section{Cohomological vanishing in genus zero}
434	
435	\begin{lemma}
436	
437	
438	\leanok
439	[Vanishing of \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
440	  \label{lem:H1_vanishing_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_genusZero}
441	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.h1_vanishing_genusZero}
442	  
443	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the proof of Theorem~1.3 (the inductive
444	  % step ``Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point.''):
445	  % the short exact sequence $0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to
446	  % k(P) \to 0\( and its tensor with \)\mathscr L(D + P)$ giving
447	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\)
448	  % (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 313).
449	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its
450	  % structure sheaf is a skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\),
451	  % which we denote by \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\)
452	  % by (II, 6.18). Therefore we have an exact sequence
453	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
454	  % Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
455	  % \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
456	  % (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
457	  % does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\))."
458	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, p.~296, the inductive step of the
459	  proof of Theorem~1.3.}
460	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
461	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
462	  \[
463	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 0,
464	  \]
465	  i.e.\ \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\).
466	\end{lemma}
467	
468	\begin{proof}
469	  
470	  Specialise Hartshorne's inductive step at \(D = 0\). The standard short
471	  exact sequence
472	  \[
473	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
474	  \]
475	  (Hartshorne~II.6.18: the ideal sheaf of the locally principal closed
476	  subscheme \(P\) is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P])\); the quotient is the structure
477	  sheaf of \(P\), which is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\)
478	  supported at \(P\) since \(P\) has residue field \(\bar k\) by closedness
479	  over the algebraically closed base \(\bar k\)). Tensoring with the
480	  locally free rank-\(1\) sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\)
481	  (REF) preserves exactness and leaves
482	  the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged, giving
483	  \[
484	    0 \to \mathcal O_C \to \mathcal O_C(P) \to k(P) \to 0
485	  \]
486	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\). Apply the long exact sequence of sheaf
487	  cohomology (REF, the project's $\Module \bar
488	  k\(-flavoured cohomology pipeline). Using that \)\dim C = 1$ kills
489	  \(H^i\) for \(i \geq 2\) (Grothendieck vanishing on a \(1\)-dimensional
490	  scheme), the long exact sequence reads
491	  \begin{multline*}
492	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^0(C, k(P)) \\
493	    \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to H^1(C, k(P)) \to 0.
494	  \end{multline*}
495	  Two ingredients close the proof:
496	
497	  \emph{(a) \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\).} This is the genus-\(0\)
498	  hypothesis: by REF,
499	  \[
500	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C),
501	  \]
502	  so \(g(C) = 0\) is exactly \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\),
503	  i.e.\ \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
504	
505	  \emph{(b) \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).} The skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) is
506	  supported at a single closed point, hence is flasque: every section
507	  on an open \(U \subset C\) either restricts to \(0\) (if \(P \notin U\)) or
508	  takes values in the stalk \(k(P)_P \cong \bar k\) (if \(P \in U\)), and
509	  restriction maps between opens of either type are surjective. A
510	  flasque sheaf on any topological space has vanishing higher
511	  cohomology (Hartshorne~III.2.5); in particular
512	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\), and \emph{a fortiori}
513	  \(H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
514	
515	  Substituting (a) and (b) into the long exact sequence collapses the
516	  segment ending at \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) to
517	  \[
518	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to 0,
519	  \]
520	  whence \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\), as required.
521	
522	  \emph{Lean reference note on the long exact sequence.} Mathlib
523	  (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the long exact sequence of
524	  \(\Ext_{\mathcal O_C}^*(\mathbf 1, -)\) at the abelian-group level
525	  (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence\_exact}); the
526	  project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured cohomology pipeline
527	  (REF) inherits the long exact sequence by
528	  forget-functor naturality. The skyscraper-cohomology vanishing
529	  \(H^i(C, k(P)) = 0\) for \(i \geq 1\) is closed by either the flasque
530	  argument (Hartshorne~III.2.5; Mathlib has the abelian-sheaf form
531	  via \texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheaf.Flasque}) or, more directly for the
532	  curve case, by the affine-cover Čech-cohomology argument on
533	  \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and a small affine open around \(P\) (the
534	  skyscraper has zero sections on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), so the Čech
535	  complex degenerates).
536	\end{proof}

538	\section{The dimension formula in genus zero}
539	
540	\begin{theorem}
541	
542	
543	\leanok
544	[\(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) on a genus-\(0\) curve]
545	  \label{thm:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
546	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.dim_eq_two_of_genusZero}
547	  
548	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, the Riemann--Roch chain inside
549	  % Example~1.3.5 applied with \(D = [P]\): $\chi(\mathscr L([P]))
550	  % = \deg([P]) + 1 - g = 1 + 1 - 0 = 2$, combined with the vanishing
551	  % \(H^1(\mathscr L([P])) = 0\) (read from
552	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 312, 314).
553	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
554	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
555	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
556	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
557	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
558	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
559	  % is the Euler characteristic
560	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
561	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295 (the
562	  \(\chi\)-identity inside the proof) and Example~1.3.5, p.~297 (the
563	  genus-\(0\) specialisation chain).}
564	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
565	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
566	  \[
567	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; 2.
568	  \]
569	\end{theorem}
570	
571	\begin{proof}
572	  
573	  Specialise the Euler-characteristic identity
574	  REF of \texttt{RR.2} to
575	  the divisor \(D = [P]\) of REF. The
576	  degree of \([P]\) is \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
577	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), and the
578	  genus hypothesis \(g(C) = 0\) (REF) gives
579	  \[
580	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(P)) \;=\; \deg([P]) + 1 - g(C)
581	    \;=\; 1 + 1 - 0 \;=\; 2.
582	  \]
583	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF as a two-term Euler
584	  characteristic on a curve, this rewrites as
585	  \[
586	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
587	    \,-\, \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
588	    \;=\; 2.
589	  \]
590	  By
591	  REF, the second
592	  term vanishes:
593	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 0\). Substituting yields
594	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), as required.
595	
596	  \emph{Alternative direct route via the long exact sequence.} The
597	  same long exact sequence used in the proof of
598	  REF computes
599	  the dimension of \(H^0\) in one stroke without invoking
600	  \texttt{RR.2}: from
601	  \[
602	    0 \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))
603	    \to H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) \to \cdots
604	  \]
605	  together with \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\)
606	  (Hartshorne~I.3.4: \(H^0\) of the structure sheaf on a proper
607	  geometrically irreducible variety over \(\bar k\) is \(\bar k\)),
608	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) (skyscraper at a \(\bar k\)-rational
609	  point), and \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0\) (genus-\(0\)), the connecting
610	  map \(H^0(C, k(P)) \to H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) has zero codomain and the
611	  segment yields the short exact sequence
612	  \[
613	    0 \to \bar k \to H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \to \bar k \to 0
614	  \]
615	  of \(\bar k\)-vector spaces, whence $\dim_{\bar k}
616	  H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 1 + 1 = 2$. This alternative route does
617	  not consume the Euler-characteristic identity of \texttt{RR.2} and
618	  could be substituted at the formalisation stage; the chapter uses
619	  \texttt{RR.2} as written above to keep the dependency graph in
620	  line with the iter-173 RR sub-build sequencing.
621	\end{proof}

623	\section{Existence of a non-constant rational function}
624	
625	\begin{corollary}
626	
627	
628	\leanok
629	[A genus-\(0\) curve admits a non-constant rational function with a
630	single simple pole]
631	  \label{cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
632	  % Alias label consumed under the shorter name by
633	  % \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalCurveIso.tex} (RR.4):
634	  \label{cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero}
635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists_nonconstant_genusZero}
636	  
637	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Exercise~1.1 (the existence of a
638	  % nonconstant rational function regular everywhere except at one
639	  % point on a genus-\(0\) curve, as a direct consequence of the
640	  % Riemann--Roch dimension formula) (read from
641	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
642	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "1.1. Let \(X\) be a curve, and let \(P \in X\) be a
643	  % point. Then there exists a nonconstant rational function $f \in
644	  % K(X)\(, which is regular everywhere except at \)P$."
645	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Exercise~1.1, p.~297.}
646	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
647	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
648	  there exists a non-constant rational function \(f \in K(C)\) such that:
649	  \begin{itemize}
650	    \item \(f\) is regular on \(C \setminus \{P\}\), i.e.\ \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
651	      for every closed point \(Q \in C\) with \(Q \neq P\);
652	    \item \(f\) has at most a simple pole at \(P\), i.e.\
653	      \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\);
654	    \item \(f \notin \bar k\), equivalently
655	      \(\div(f) \neq 0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\).
656	  \end{itemize}
657	  In particular, the pair \((1, f)\) spans
658	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) as a \(\bar k\)-vector space.
659	\end{corollary}
660	
661	\begin{proof}
662	  
663	  By
664	  REF,
665	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\). The constant subspace
666	  \(\bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\), obtained from the
667	  structural inclusion $\bar k \cong H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
668	  \hookrightarrow H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))$ (the rational function
669	  \(f = 1\), with \(\div(1) + [P] = [P] \geq 0\), under the
670	  identification of
671	  REF), is a one-dimensional
672	  \(\bar k\)-vector subspace. Since the ambient space has dimension \(2\),
673	  the quotient \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) / \bar k \cdot 1\) is a
674	  non-zero one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-vector space; any lift to
675	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) of a non-zero element of the quotient is
676	  an element of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k \cdot 1\).
677	
678	  Choose such a lift $s \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) \setminus \bar k
679	  \cdot 1$. Under the identification of
680	  REF the section
681	  \(s\) corresponds to a rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\)
682	  satisfying \(\div(f) + [P] \geq 0\), equivalently \(\ord_Q(f) \geq 0\)
683	  for every \(Q \neq P\) and \(\ord_P(f) \geq -1\). The choice
684	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) means \(f\) is not the image of a constant,
685	  so \(f \notin \bar k\) (every non-zero element of
686	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) \cong \bar k\) corresponds, under the
687	  identification, to a constant element of \(K(C)\)). The three bullets
688	  of the statement are now immediate.
689	
690	  Spanning. Linear independence of \(1\) and \(f\) over \(\bar k\) in
691	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\) is the same condition as
692	  \(s \notin \bar k \cdot 1\) above; combined with the dimension count
693	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\), the pair \((1, f)\) is a
694	  \(\bar k\)-basis of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P))\).
695	
696	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
697	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.lineBundleAtClosedPoint.exists\_nonconstant\_genusZero}
698	  returns an element \(f\) of the project's function-field carrier
699	  \(K(C)\) together with proofs of regularity on \(C \setminus \{P\}\) and
700	  the simple-pole bound at \(P\), packaged in the same shape as
701	  REF's output. The
702	  non-constancy is recorded as a separate field; the spanning
703	  statement is downstream and is consumed by \texttt{RR.4} via
704	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}.
705	\end{proof}

888	/-! ## §2. Global sections as the Riemann–Roch space `L([P])`
889	
890	Hartshorne II §7 Proposition 7.7 identifies the global sections of
891	`ℒ(D_0)` with the rational functions `f ∈ K(X)^×` satisfying
892	`div(f) ≥ −D_0` (plus the zero section). Specialised to `D_0 = [P]` on a
893	curve, the condition `div(f) + [P] ≥ 0` rewrites coordinate-wise as
894	`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`. -/
895	
896	/-- **Forward direction of `globalSections_iff` (Hartshorne II.7.7(b)).**
897	
898	Given `f ∈ K(C)^×` with `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for every prime divisor `Q ≠ P`
899	and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`, the rational function `f` lifts to a global section
900	`s ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` whose image under the canonical inclusion
901	`𝒪_C(P) ↪ 𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` equals `f`. Hartshorne's construction inside the
902	proof of Proposition 7.7(b) (p. 157) defines this section locally on the
903	affine cover witnessing the Cartier-divisor structure of `[P]`: on a
904	neighbourhood `U_i` of `P` the section is `f · f_P` (in
905	`𝒪_C(P)(U_i) = f_P⁻¹ · 𝒪_C(U_i)`, this is `(f · f_P) · f_P⁻¹ = f`);
906	on the complement `C ∖ {P}` the section is `f` directly (the order
907	conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` guarantee `f ∈ 𝒪_C(U) = 𝒪_C(P)(U)`

126	noncomputable def Scheme.eulerCharacteristic
127	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
128	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
129	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
130	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
131	    (F : Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
132	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)) : ℤ :=
133	  (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 0) : ℤ)
134	    - (Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar F 1) : ℤ)

136	/-! ## §2. The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor (sibling chapter)
137	
138	The honest construction of `𝒪_C(D)` lives in the sibling chapter `RR.3`
139	(`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, `AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/OcOfD.lean`),
140	where the locally-principal ideal sheaves of closed points are glued
141	into an invertible `𝒪_C`-module. **iter-183 Lane K** opened that file
142	with the typed-`sorry` pin `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf` plus the three
143	immediate corollaries `sheafOf_zero`, `sheafOf_singlePoint`, and
144	`sheafOf_ses_single_add`. **iter-183 Lane H** (this file) retires the
145	former local `sheafOf` typed-`sorry` placeholder (previously at L168 in
146	the iter-174 skeleton) by importing `OcOfD.lean`; downstream pins (the
147	`ℓ`-invariant, the χ-identity, the genus-`0` Riemann–Roch formula) now
148	reference the chapter's canonical `sheafOf` directly. -/
149	
150	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
151	
152	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
153	  {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
154	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
155	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
156	
157	/-! ## §3. The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor -/
158	
159	/-- **The `ℓ`-invariant of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper curve
160	`C / k̄`.**
161	
162	By definition,
163	`ℓ(D) := dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(D)) ∈ ℕ`,
164	the `k̄`-dimension of the space of global sections of the invertible sheaf
165	`𝒪_C(D)` associated to `D`. Finiteness of `ℓ(D)` is a consequence of the
166	coherent-cohomology finiteness theorem on a proper `k̄`-scheme
167	(Hartshorne II.5.19 / III.5.2), the same input that backs the finiteness
168	of `genus C`.
169	
170	The classical *complete linear system* `|D|` has `k̄`-projective dimension
171	`ℓ(D) − 1`; the chapter never uses this projective interpretation —
172	`ℓ(D)` is the only quantity consumed by the Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain.
173	
174	Blueprint reference: `def:l_invariant` (Hartshorne IV.1 p. 295,
175	"We denote `dim_k H⁰(X, 𝒪(D))` by `l(D)`"). -/
176	noncomputable def l (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) : ℕ :=
177	  Module.finrank kbar (Scheme.HModule kbar (sheafOf (C := C) D) 0)
178	
179	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
180	
181	/-! ## §4. The χ-identity: `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g`
182	
183	The bridge from the structural Euler characteristic to the arithmetic
184	degree of a divisor. Hartshorne IV.1 Theorem 1.3 reduces Riemann–Roch to
185	this identity; the proof is the inductive `D ↔ D + [P]` step (additivity
186	of χ on the closed-point short exact sequence, with base case `D = 0`
187	giving `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` from `dim H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` and the definition of
188	the genus). The closure of the body is iter-175+ work after the
189	`Euler-characteristic additivity on a short exact sequence`-style
190	project-side helper is supplied and the `RR.3` sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` has a
191	body.
192	
193	**Iter-181 Lane H factoring.** Both Hartshorne IV.1.3 inputs (the base
194	case `χ(𝒪_C) = 1 − g` and the inductive step `χ(𝒪_C(D + Y)) = χ(𝒪_C(D))
195	+ n`) intrinsically reference the body of `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf`,
196	which is still a typed-`sorry` placeholder waiting on the sibling
197	chapter `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`). We therefore factor the proof
198	into two named substantive helper lemmas (3-tier disclosure: **honest
199	named-sorry helpers** — each helper has a substantive type encoding a
200	nontrivial mathematical claim about the `sheafOf` line bundle whose
201	closure is downstream of the `RR.3` body):
202	
203	1. `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero` — base case `χ(sheafOf 0) = 1 − g`,
204	   which on closure of `RR.3` reduces to `sheafOf 0 = toModuleKSheaf C`
205	   plus the Hartshorne I.3.4 input `dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1` plus the
206	   definition of the genus.
207	2. `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add` — inductive step
208	   `χ(sheafOf (Finsupp.single Y n + D)) = χ(sheafOf D) + n` for any
209	   `Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor` and `n : ℤ`. On closure of `RR.3` this
210	   reduces to the Hartshorne IV.1.3 SES additivity argument iterated
211	   `|n|` times (with sign for `n < 0`).
212	
213	The main theorem then closes by induction on the `Finsupp`-structure of
214	`D : C.left.WeilDivisor` (via `Finsupp.induction`), using the two
215	helpers and the additivity of `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree`. -/
216	
217	/-- **Hartshorne I.3.4 bridge** (Lane H helper A — iter-183).
218	On a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the global
219	sections of the structure sheaf form a one-dimensional `k̄`-vector space:
220	`dim_{k̄} H⁰(C, 𝒪_C) = 1`.
221	
222	This is the Hartshorne~I.3.4 statement "for any projective variety `X`
223	over an algebraically closed field `k`, `H⁰(X, 𝒪_X) ≅ k`" specialised to
224	the project's `ModuleCat k̄`-flavoured cohomology pipeline
225	(`Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 0`). The closure lives
226	in the project's `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK` chapter (the H⁰
227	bridge from the constant-sheaf adjunction) and is gated on the
228	remaining cohomology-API work in that file.
229	
230	**iter-183 Lane H status** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry. Body iter-184+
231	via the `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK` H⁰-bridge. -/
232	private theorem Scheme.finrank_H0_toModuleKSheaf_eq_one
233	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
234	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
235	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
236	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left] :
237	    Module.finrank kbar
238	        (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C) 0) = 1 := by
239	  -- Step 1: H⁰-bridge LinearEquiv chain (existing project infrastructure).

56:3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus`
69:The chapter's proof of `eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus`
687:theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus
728:`eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus` to `g = 0`,
737:theorem Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero
748:  have h := Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus C D

680	
681	**Iter-181 Lane H status** — the main theorem is closed by induction on
682	the `Finsupp` structure of `D`, consuming the two `sheafOf`-side helper
683	sorries `eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero` (base case) and
684	`eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add` (inductive step). The body
685	itself is honest; its `sorryAx`-transitivity is the inevitable
686	consequence of `sheafOf` still being a typed sorry waiting on `RR.3`. -/
687	theorem Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus
688	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
689	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
690	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
691	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
692	    (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) :
693	    Scheme.eulerCharacteristic C (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D)
694	      = (Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D) + 1 - (AlgebraicGeometry.genus C : ℤ) := by
695	  -- Unfold `WeilDivisor` to expose the underlying `Finsupp` structure so
696	  -- `Finsupp.induction` applies directly.
697	  unfold Scheme.WeilDivisor at D
698	  induction D using Finsupp.induction with
699	  | zero =>
700	    -- D = 0: χ(sheafOf 0) = 1 - g by helper 1, and degree 0 = 0.
701	    exact (Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_zero C).trans
702	      (by simp [Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree])
703	  | single_add Y n D' _hY _hn hD' =>
704	    -- D = single Y n + D': use helper 2 then the inductive hypothesis.
705	    rw [Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add, hD']
706	    -- Goal: (degree D' + 1 - g) + n = degree (single Y n + D') + 1 - g.
707	    have hdeg : Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree
708	        ((Finsupp.single Y n + D' : C.left.WeilDivisor))
709	        = n + Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D' := by
710	      change ((Finsupp.single Y n + D' : C.left.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)).sum
711	          (fun _ z => z) = _
712	      classical
713	      rw [Finsupp.sum_add_index (fun _ _ => rfl) (fun _ _ _ _ => rfl)]
714	      simp [Finsupp.sum_single_index]
715	      rfl
716	    linarith
717	
718	/-! ## §5. The Riemann–Roch formula in genus zero -/
719	
720	/-- **Riemann–Roch in genus zero (Hartshorne IV.1 Example 1.3.5).**
721	
722	Let `C` be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over the
723	algebraically closed field `k̄` with `g(C) = 0`, and let `D ∈ Div(C)` with
724	`deg D ≥ 0`. Then
725	`ℓ(D) = deg(D) + 1`.
726	
727	The proof specialises the χ-identity
728	`eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus` to `g = 0`,
729	unfolds `χ` via `def:eulerChar_curve`, and absorbs the `H¹`-vanishing
730	hypothesis (named premise `_hH1`: `H¹(C, 𝒪_C(D)) = 0`, which is the
731	`H¹`-vanishing of a non-negative-degree invertible sheaf on a
732	genus-`0` curve, to be discharged by `RR.3` once `𝒪_C(D)` has a body and
733	the cohomology of `𝒪_{ℙ¹}(d)` is computed).
734	
735	Blueprint reference: `thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero` (Hartshorne IV.1
736	Example 1.3.5, p. 297). -/
737	theorem Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero
738	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
739	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
740	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
741	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
742	    (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) (_hg : AlgebraicGeometry.genus C = 0)
743	    (_hdeg : (0 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D)
744	    (_hH1 : Module.finrank kbar
745	      (Scheme.HModule kbar (Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf (C := C) D) 1) = 0) :
746	    (Scheme.WeilDivisor.l (C := C) D : ℤ)
747	      = Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree D + 1 := by
748	  have h := Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus C D
749	  simp only [Scheme.eulerCharacteristic, _hg, _hH1,
750	    Nat.cast_zero, sub_zero] at h
751	  exact h
752	
753	end AlgebraicGeometry

441:noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
445:    Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal (↑u : X.functionField) (Units.ne_zero u))
503:theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]

375	private theorem packaging Hartshorne 6.1; its body is a Mathlib-pending gap
376	(see chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5 sub-build note) and is left as
377	a `sorry` for an iter-178+ Mathlib-upstream PR. -/
378	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
379	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField)
380	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
381	  Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
382	    (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
383	    (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)
384	
385	/-- **The coefficient of `principal f hf` at a prime divisor `Y` is the order of
386	`f` along `Y`.** This is the basic structural unfolding of the
387	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` packaging in `principal`; one-line via
388	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe` from `Mathlib.Data.Finsupp.Defs`.
389	
390	iter-193 substrate helper for the Lane I body close
391	(`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`). -/
392	lemma principal_apply [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
393	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0)
394	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) :
395	    (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y =
396	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
397	  change (Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
398	      (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
399	      (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)) Y = _
400	  rw [Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe]
401	
402	/-- **`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y 1 = 0`** — the order of the constant
403	function `1` is `0` at every prime divisor. Direct from
404	`map_one` of `Ring.ordFrac` + `WithZero.log_one`.
405	
406	iter-193 substrate helper for further structural results
407	(`principal_one_eq_zero` and similar). -/
408	@[simp]
409	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_one
410	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
411	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
412	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)] :
413	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (1 : X.functionField) = 0 := by
414	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
415	  rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
416	
417	/-- **The principal divisor of the constant function `1` is the zero
418	divisor.** A direct consequence of `Scheme.RationalMap.order_one`. -/
419	@[simp]
420	lemma principal_one [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
421	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
422	    principal (1 : X.functionField) one_ne_zero = 0 := by
423	  change (_ : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) = (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
424	  apply Finsupp.ext
425	  intro Y
426	  rw [principal_apply]
427	  exact Scheme.RationalMap.order_one Y
428	
429	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
430	
431	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
432	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
433	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
434	
435	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
436	
437	iter-177 body: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities
438	`v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`. The per-`Y` identities live in
439	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` via `Ring.ordFrac` (a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero
440	hom) and `WithZero.log_mul` / `WithZero.log_one`. -/
441	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
442	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
443	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor where
444	  toFun u :=
445	    Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal (↑u : X.functionField) (Units.ne_zero u))
446	  map_one' := by
447	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑1 _) = 1.
448	    -- Use `← ofAdd_zero` to rewrite RHS as `Multiplicative.ofAdd 0`,
449	    -- then `congr 1` reduces to `principal ↑1 _ = 0` (Finsupp equality),
450	    -- and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate identity, which
451	    -- closes by unfolding `order` to `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 1) = 0`
452	    -- via `map_one` and `WithZero.log_one`.
453	    rw [← ofAdd_zero]
454	    congr 1
455	    apply Finsupp.ext
456	    intro Y
457	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y
458	        (((1 : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField)) = 0
459	    rw [Units.val_one]
460	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
461	    rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]

    [∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
        Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point)]
    (P : C.left) (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C.left))

      (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ f ∧
      Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal (X := C.left) f hf ≠ 0 :=

    (dim_eq_two_of_genusZero P _hP hPcoh _hg)

input.old_string:
/-! ## §5. A non-constant rational function with at most a simple pole at `P`

The two-dimensionality of `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` and the one-dimensional
constant subspace `k̄ · 1` give a non-zero quotient `H⁰/k̄`. Any lift
of a non-zero element of the quotient is, under the identification of
`globalSections_iff`, a non-constant rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with
the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`.
This is the seed of `RR.4` (the morphism `C → ℙ¹` produced by
`Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` from the basis `(1, f)`). -/

/-! ## §5. A non-constant rational function with at most a simple pole at `P`

The two-dimensionality of `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C(P))` and the one-dimensional
constant subspace `k̄ · 1` give a non-zero quotient `H⁰/k̄`. Any lift
of a non-zero element of the quotient is, under the identification of
`globalSections_iff`, a non-constant rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with
the order conditions `ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P` and `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`.
This is the seed of `RR.4` (the morphism `C → ℙ¹` produced by
`Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` from the basis `(1, f)`). -/

/-- **Existence of a non-constant rational function with the order
conditions and nonzero principal divisor, from `dim H⁰(𝒪_C(P)) = 2`.**

iter-194 Lane A first body push — named substrate helper carving the
linear-algebra + principal-divisor content out of
`exists_nonconstant_genusZero` (so the body of the latter is a one-line
invocation supplied with the dimension count from `dim_eq_two_of_genusZero`).

The helper carries the substantive content independent of the genus
hypothesis (only the dim H⁰ = 2 input is consumed):

1. **Linear algebra.** The structural inclusion of constants
   `kbar →ₗ[kbar] H⁰(𝒪_C(P))` (`LinearMap.toSpanSingleton` applied to the
   distinguished section `s₁` built from `globalSections_iff_mp` at
   `f = 1` with the trivial order conditions
   `(∀ Q ≠ P, 0 ≤ ord_Q 1 = 0) ∧ (-1 ≤ ord_P 1 = 0)`) has 1-dimensional
   image, so by `hdim : dim H⁰ = 2 > 1` the image is a strict subspace and
   a section `s ∈ H⁰ ∖ kbar · s₁` exists.
2. **Function-field extraction.** Set `f := toFunctionField P hP hPcoh s`,
   non-zero because `s ∉ kbar · s₁` and `toFunctionField` is kbar-linear
   on the constant subspace (the linearity ensures
   `s ∈ kbar · s₁ ⟺ f ∈ kbar · 1 = image of algebraMap kbar K(C)`).
3. **Order conditions.** Apply `globalSections_iff_mpr P hP f hf hPcoh ⟨s, rfl⟩`
   to read off the pair `(∀ Q ≠ P, 0 ≤ ord_Q f) ∧ (-1 ≤ ord_P f)`.
4. **Principal divisor non-vanishing.** Use the Stacks 02P0 / Hartshorne
   II.6.7 statement: a rational function `f ∈ K(C)` with `principal f = 0`
   (no zeros or poles at any prime divisor) is a global unit
   `f ∈ Γ(C, 𝒪_C)^× = k̄^×` on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
   curve over `k̄`. Contrapositive: `f ∉ k̄` ⟹ `principal f ≠ 0`. The
   non-constancy from step 2 supplies the hypothesis.

Substrate gates:
- Step 1's linear-algebra extraction is mechanical from `hdim` once the
  constant-inclusion `kbar → H⁰` is set up (and the `s₁` distinguished
  section exists via `globalSections_iff_mp`).
- Step 4's principal-divisor non-vanishing is the standard
  Hartshorne~II.6.7 / Stacks 02P0 argument; it consumes properness +
  integrality of `C.left` and the function-field identification of
  `Γ(C, 𝒪_C^×) ≅ k̄^×` from `IsAlgClosed kbar`.

This helper is the natural single point at which the `exists_nonconstant_genusZero`
substantive content lives, isolated from the dimension-count hypothesis
(which is itself isolated in `dim_eq_two_of_genusZero` ↦
`h0_sub_h1_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_eq_two` ↦ `h1_vanishing_genusZero`).

Blueprint reference: `cor:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_exists_nonconstant_genusZero`
(Hartshorne IV.1 Exercise 1.1, p. 297). -/

    [∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
        Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (C.left.presheaf.stalk Q.point)]
    (P : C.left) (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C.left))
    (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1)
    (_hdim : Module.finrank kbar
        (Scheme.HModule kbar
          (lineBundleAtClosedPoint (C := C) P hP hPcoh) 0) = 2) :
    ∃ (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0),
      (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ≠ P →
          0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q f) ∧
      (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order ⟨P, hPcoh⟩ f ∧
      Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal (X := C.left) f hf ≠ 0 := by

  -- distinguished section spans the constant subspace `kbar · s₁ ⊆ H⁰`,

  -- ambient H⁰ (by `_hdim`). The remaining substantive content is the
  -- linear-algebra extraction of a non-constant section + the
  -- principal-divisor non-vanishing (Stacks 02P0).

      (∀ Q : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Q.point ≠ P →
          0 ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order Q (1 : C.left.functionField)) ∧
        (-1 : ℤ) ≤ Scheme.RationalMap.order ⟨P, hPcoh⟩
          (1 : C.left.functionField) := by
    refine ⟨fun Q _ => ?_, ?_⟩
    · rw [Scheme.RationalMap.order_one]
    · rw [Scheme.RationalMap.order_one]; norm_num
  -- Distinguished constant section: `s₁` is the global section of `𝒪_C(P)`
  -- whose underlying rational function is `1 ∈ K(C)`.
  obtain ⟨_s₁, _hs₁⟩ :=
    globalSections_iff_mp (C := C) P hP (1 : C.left.functionField)
      one_ne_zero hPcoh h1_orders
  -- Remaining substantive gap: linear-algebra extraction of a section
  -- `s ∉ kbar · s₁` (using `_hdim` and the 1-dim constant subspace
  -- `kbar · s₁`), extract `f := toFunctionField s` with `f ≠ 0` and
  -- `f ∉ kbar`, derive order conditions via `globalSections_iff_mpr`,
  -- and close `principal f ≠ 0` from non-constancy (Stacks 02P0).
  sorry

### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Carve out the χ-arithmetic into a named substrate helper

## exists_nonconstant_genusZero (L1361 → body closed)

### Attempt 1

  into a named substrate helper `exists_nonconstant_rational_from_dim_eq_two`

- **Result:** RESOLVED (body now compiles without bare sorry;
  transitively gated on the new substrate helper).

  helper without needing the genus hypothesis in scope.

  of a non-constant section; `globalSections_iff_mpr` to extract order
  conditions; Stacks 02P0 for principal-divisor non-vanishing).

## h0_sub_h1_lineBundleAtClosedPoint_eq_two (L1209) — new helper

### Status

- Substantive content: bridge `lineBundleAtClosedPoint P hP hPcoh` to the

## exists_nonconstant_rational_from_dim_eq_two (L1323) — new helper

### Status

### Partial setup committed

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
153

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128	
129	Before stopping:
130	
131	1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with: current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
132	2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles (use scoped `sorry` for stuck parts if needed).

10	/-!
11	# The rational-curve isomorphism `C ≅ ℙ¹` (RR.4)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.4** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma pinned in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity:290`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ⇒ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through the four sub-build chapters:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)`, the degree
22	  map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`, and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and Riemann–Roch in genus zero.
25	- `RR.3` (`OCofP.lean`): the line bundle `𝒪_C([P])` of a closed point and
26	  the existence of a non-constant rational function
27	  `f ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C([P])) ∖ k̄ · 1`.
28	- **`RR.4` (this file)**: the global classification — the linear-system
29	  morphism `C ⟶ ℙ¹` produced from `(1, f)` (Pin 1), its degree identified
30	  via the pole divisor (Pin 2), and the degree-`1` ⇒ isomorphism step
31	  (Pin 3). The headline target `AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1`
32	  (Pin 4) is the existing pinned declaration at
33	  `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290` — see §0 below.
34	
35	## Status (iter-177 file-skeleton; iter-181 Pin 3 signature refinement)
36	
37	This file was originally the **iter-177 Lane 8** file-skeleton. Each
38	of the three new pinned declarations carries the *intended*
39	substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
40	`blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex`) with a
41	`sorry` body. Bodies are iter-182+ work after the RR.1/RR.2/RR.3
42	chain matures (in particular `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero`
43	and the `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C([P]))`-non-constant existence corollary of `RR.3`).
44	
45	iter-181 Lane I refined the Pin 3 signature of
46	`iso_of_degree_one`: the abstract function-field-iso existence
47	hypothesis is replaced by the `[Algebra K(C') K(C)]` typeclass binder
48	+ `Module.finrank K(C') K(C) = 1` equation, matching what the
49	iter-182+ body needs (per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`
50	Decision 2 DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY).
51	
52	The 3 new pinned declarations are:
53	
54	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections` — from a
55	   graded `k̄`-algebra hom `k̄[X₀, X₁] →+* Γ(X, ⊤)` whose image of the
56	   irrelevant ideal generates the unit ideal, produce the morphism
57	   `X ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` via `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` lifted to
58	   the slice category over `Spec k̄`.
59	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` — for a
60	   non-constant morphism `φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` from a smooth
61	   proper curve, extract a positive-degree Weil divisor on `C` (the
62	   pole divisor `φ^*[∞]`).
63	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one` — a non-constant
64	   morphism between smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves
65	   whose induced function-field map is a `k̄`-algebra iso is an
66	   isomorphism of schemes (Hartshorne I.6.12 specialised to
67	   degree-`1`).
68	
69	The headline pin (Pin 4) is satisfied by the existing AVR.lean target.
70	
71	## Note on type expressivity
72	
73	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
74	each pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
75	
76	* `morphismToP1OfGlobalSections` returns a *concrete* morphism
77	  `X ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` of `Over (Spec k̄)`, not `Iso.refl _` or a
78	  reflexive placeholder; the input data is the exact pair
79	  `(f, hf)` consumed by Mathlib's `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections`.
80	* `morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` asserts the existence of a
81	  positive-integer degree `d` together with a Weil divisor `D` on `C`
82	  realising it — the *named substantive content* of the degree-via-pole
83	  identification. Unfolds to `∃ d D, 0 < d ∧ D.degree = d`, not `True`.
84	* `iso_of_degree_one` asserts `Nonempty (C ≅ C')` from the
85	  non-constant hypothesis together with the *substantive* degree-`1`
86	  hypothesis `Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1`
87	  (paired with an `[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]` typeclass
88	  binder for the canonical `φ`-induced function-field map). The
89	  `Nonempty`-wrapped iso is the exact downstream-consumer shape,
90	  matching the `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` headline signature in AVR.lean.
91	  This signature was refined in iter-181 Lane I from the iter-177
92	  file-skeleton placeholder `Nonempty (C'.ff ≃+* C.ff)` to make the
93	  degree-`1`-induced-by-`φ` hypothesis the substantive content; see
94	  `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md` Decision 2.
95	
96	Unfolding any pinned declaration exposes the named substantive content
97	(the concrete `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections`-derived morphism, the
98	existence-of-positive-degree-divisor statement, the existence of a
99	scheme isomorphism); no `Iso.refl _` / empty-content `proof_wanted` /
100	`Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` placeholders are used.
101	
102	## Curve conventions
103	
104	Following the project (cf. `AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`,
105	`Jacobian.lean`, `OCofP.lean`), a **smooth proper geometrically
106	irreducible curve over `k̄`** carries:
107	
108	- `[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]`,
109	- `[IsProper C.hom]`,
110	- `[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]`.
111	
112	These instances are supplied by `inferInstance` at the call site. The
113	`[IsIntegral C.left]` / `[IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]` instances
114	required by the Weil-divisor / `RationalMap.order` API are threaded
115	explicitly per-lemma where consumed.
116	
117	## References
118	
119	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex`
120	(~535 LOC, 4 pins). Source: Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*,
121	IV §1 Example 1.3.5, p. 297 (the genus-`0` ⇒ `ℙ¹` classification);
122	IV §2 opening paragraph, p. 299 (degree of a finite morphism of
123	curves); I §6 Corollary 6.12, p. 45 (function-field ⇔ smooth projective
124	curve equivalence of categories); II §7 Theorem 7.1 (morphisms to
125	`ℙⁿ` via global sections). Stacks Project tag `0AVX` (degree-`1`
126	finite morphism between integral normal schemes is an isomorphism).

165	/-! ## §1. Pin 1 — the morphism `X ⟶ ℙ¹` from a globally-generating pair
166	(`morphismToP1OfGlobalSections`)
167	
168	Hartshorne II Theorem 7.1, specialised to `ℙ¹` over `k̄`. Given a graded
169	`k̄`-algebra homomorphism `f : k̄[X₀, X₁] →+* Γ(X, ⊤)` whose image of the
170	irrelevant ideal `(X₀, X₁)` generates the unit ideal of `Γ(X, ⊤)`, the
171	construction `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` of Mathlib's
172	`AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic` produces a morphism
173	`X.left ⟶ Proj 𝒜 = ProjectiveLineBarScheme k̄`. Lifting to the slice
174	category over `Spec k̄` (via `Over.homMk` with a section-condition
175	proof) yields the pinned morphism
176	`X ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄ : Over (Spec (.of kbar))`. -/
177	
178	/-- **The morphism `X ⟶ ℙ¹_{k̄}` produced from a globally-generating pair of
179	homogeneous sections** (Hartshorne II Theorem 7.1; Mathlib's
180	`AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` specialised to the standard
181	ℕ-grading on `k̄[X₀, X₁]`).
182	
183	Given a graded `k̄`-algebra homomorphism `f : k̄[X₀, X₁] →+* Γ(X, ⊤)`, a
184	`k̄`-algebra compatibility witness `halg` (recording that the composite
185	`kbar → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar → Γ(X.left, ⊤)` agrees with the natural
186	algebra map `kbar → Γ(X.left, ⊤)` induced by `X.hom`), and a proof `hf`
187	that the image of the irrelevant ideal `(X₀, X₁) ⊆ k̄[X₀, X₁]` generates
188	the unit ideal of `Γ(X, ⊤)`, this constructs the unique morphism
189	`φ : X ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` characterised on basic-opens by
190	`φ ⁻¹ᵁ D₊(X_i) = X.basicOpen (f X_i)` (the "no-common-zero" expression of
191	Hartshorne's morphism condition). The composite
192	`φ.left ≫ Proj.toSpecZero _ = X.hom`, witnessing the `Over (Spec k̄)`
193	section condition.
194	
195	The input data `(f, halg, hf)` is the standard Hartshorne II §7 packaging
196	of "a pair of global sections `s₀, s₁ ∈ H⁰(X, ℒ)` of an invertible sheaf
197	`ℒ` without common zeros": `f` is the graded `k̄`-algebra map sending
198	`X_i ↦ s_i ∈ Γ(X, ℒ) ⊂ Γ(X, ⊤)` (after a choice of trivialisation
199	identifying `H⁰(X, ℒ)` with a submodule of `Γ(X, ⊤)` over the affine
200	cover `X = D(s₀) ∪ D(s₁)`); `halg` is the `kbar`-algebra-compatibility
201	condition (without which the section condition over `Spec kbar` cannot
202	be derived); `hf` is the no-common-zero condition.
203	
204	The construction invokes `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` on the standard
205	grading `projectiveLineBarGrading k̄` (the `ℕ`-grading on
206	`MvPolynomial (Fin 2) k̄` by total degree, with `GradedRing` instance
207	via `MvPolynomial.gradedAlgebra`), then wraps in `Over.homMk` with the
208	section condition proof. The section condition chases through
209	`Proj.fromOfGlobalSections_toSpecZero` plus
210	`IsScalarTower kbar (𝒜 0) (MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar)` to reduce to
211	`X.left.toSpecΓ ≫ Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (f.comp MvPolynomial.C)) = X.hom`,
212	which the `halg` hypothesis closes via `Scheme.toSpecΓ_naturality` and
213	`toSpecΓ_SpecMap_ΓSpecIso_inv` (cf. the analogous chain in
214	`Genus0BaseObjects/Points.lean:pointOfVec`).
215	
216	Blueprint reference: `lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections`
217	(Hartshorne II Theorem 7.1, II §7). -/
218	noncomputable def morphismToP1OfGlobalSections
219	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
220	    (X : Over (Spec (.of kbar)))
221	    (f : MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar →+* Γ(X.left, ⊤))
222	    (_halg :
223	      f.comp (algebraMap kbar (MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar)) =
224	        X.hom.appTop.hom.comp (Scheme.ΓSpecIso (CommRingCat.of kbar)).inv.hom)
225	    (_hf :
226	      Ideal.map f
227	          (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar)).toIdeal = ⊤) :
228	    X ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar kbar :=
229	  -- Underlying scheme morphism: invoke Mathlib's
230	  -- `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` on `projectiveLineBarGrading kbar`.
231	  -- The result `X.left ⟶ Proj 𝒜 = ProjectiveLineBarScheme kbar` is
232	  -- then wrapped via `Over.homMk` with the section condition
233	  -- `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections … ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.hom = X.hom`.
234	  Over.homMk
235	    (Proj.fromOfGlobalSections (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar) f _hf) <| by

237	    --   `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections … ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.hom = X.hom`
238	    -- where `ProjectiveLineBar.hom = Proj.toSpecZero _ ≫ Spec.map (algebraMap kbar ↥(𝒜 0))`.

240	        (MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) :=
241	      IsScalarTower.of_algebraMap_eq
242	        (R := kbar) (S := ↥(projectiveLineBarGrading kbar 0))
243	        (A := MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) (fun _ => rfl)
244	    change Proj.fromOfGlobalSections _ _ _ ≫ Proj.toSpecZero _ ≫ Spec.map _ = _
245	    rw [← Category.assoc, Proj.fromOfGlobalSections_toSpecZero, Category.assoc,
246	      ← Spec.map_comp, ← CommRingCat.ofHom_comp, RingHom.comp_assoc,
247	      ← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_eq kbar, MvPolynomial.algebraMap_eq]

251	        (Scheme.ΓSpecIso (CommRingCat.of kbar)).inv ≫ X.hom.appTop := by

257	      Category.assoc, toSpecΓ_SpecMap_ΓSpecIso_inv, Category.comp_id]
